will help new Julia programmers who don't know about `JULIA_NUM_THREADS=auto` get fast execution. It also symbolizes how Julia supports the increasing parallelism of modern technical computing. Anybody who needs sequential execution, e.g. for debugging, can use `JULIA_NUM_THREADS=1`.

- [Rust's Rayon](https://github.com/rayon-rs/rayon/blob/master/FAQ.md#how-many-threads-will-rayon-spawn) uses the number of logical cores by default.

- [Intel® Cilk™ Plus](http://wwweic.eri.u-tokyo.ac.jp/computer/manual/eic2015/doc/intel/en_US/compiler_c/GUID-85B8EB6D-A49C-4D44-B58E-88DCA85FBE23.htm) uses the number of hardware threads.

- [Crystal](https://crystal-lang.org/2019/09/06/parallelism-in-crystal.html) defaults to 4.

- [Fortran OMP](https://docs.oracle.com/cd/E19205-01/819-5262/aeuip/index.html) defaults to 1.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43672/reactions","total_count":10,"+1":8,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":2,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43672/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2511891845","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43672#issuecomment-2511891845","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43672","id":2511891845,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6VuG2F","user":{"login":"jpsamaroo","id":17912843,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE3OTEyODQz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/17912843?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo","html_url":"https://github.com/jpsamaroo","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-02T15:45:24Z","updated_at":"2024-12-02T15:45:24Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"DistributedNext.jl has seen thread-safety changes merged, so users have options if we wanted to switch this on.

That said, if we're going to block this on Distributed.jl becoming thread-safe, then we should have someone from the core team help review https://github.com/JuliaLang/Distributed.jl/pull/101. The changes are not really too complicated, especially if you just review everything before https://github.com/JuliaLang/Distributed.jl/pull/101/commits/0377cda9b4bfa49205305ca7a769d0d6bd75f8f8 (since this commit and those following it are not necessary to improve the thread-safety of Distributed).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2511891845/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

- [Rust's Rayon](https://github.com/rayon-rs/rayon/blob/master/FAQ.md#how-many-threads-will-rayon-spawn) uses the number of logical cores by default.

- [Intel® Cilk™ Plus](http://wwweic.eri.u-tokyo.ac.jp/computer/manual/eic2015/doc/intel/en_US/compiler_c/GUID-85B8EB6D-A49C-4D44-B58E-88DCA85FBE23.htm) uses the number of hardware threads.

- [Crystal](https://crystal-lang.org/2019/09/06/parallelism-in-crystal.html) defaults to 4.

BigFloat"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":5,"created_at":"2024-11-13T12:58:18Z","updated_at":"2024-11-13T17:15:15Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"body":"If you do a big computation with `BigInt`s  like

```
function test_hm(rtype,rank)
  m=[rtype(1)//(n+m) for n in 1:rank, m in 1:rank]
  one(m)==m*inv(m)
end
```
Then
`test_hm(BigInt,100)`
takes about 2s so you can interrupt it with ^C. Then if you restart the same command you get often a message like
```
double free or corruption (!prev)

[2342677] signal 6 (-6): Abandon
in expression starting at REPL[5]:1

```
You may need to do the restart/break a few times to get the error. I have a bigger computation with `BigInt` where the error is systematic after a Ctrl-C. This is on
```
Julia Version 1.11.1
Commit 8f5b7ca12ad (2024-10-16 10:53 UTC)
Build Info:
  Official https://julialang.org/ release
Platform Info:
  OS: Linux (x86_64-linux-gnu)
  CPU: 16 × 11th Gen Intel(R) Core(TM) i7-11700 @ 2.50GHz
  WORD_SIZE: 64
  LLVM: libLLVM-16.0.6 (ORCJIT, rocketlake)
Threads: 1 default, 0 interactive, 1 GC (on 16 virtual cores
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56545/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56545/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2474252893","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56545#issuecomment-2474252893","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56545","id":2474252893,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6Tehpd","user":{"login":"jpsamaroo","id":17912843,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE3OTEyODQz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/17912843?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo","html_url":"https://github.com/jpsamaroo","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-11-13T17:15:13Z","updated_at":"2024-11-13T17:15:13Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"The issue here is related to [#49541](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/49541) - basically, as @oscardssmith points out, it is *currently* not safe to interrupt basically any kind of code, because of how aggressive and non-cooperative our interrupt mechanism is. It's pretty good at stopping all kinds of code from continuing to run and subsequently returning to the REPL (to an extent), but because of how good it is at this, no guarantees can be made about the consistency of the current process' data structures (which may be in an inconsistent state as they were interrupted while working on some operation). Basically, you can interrupt just once, but you're left with a potentially garbage Julia session.

The PR I linked would resolve this, not through magically making the current mechanism better, but by providing an alternative cooperative mechanism for allowing running code to gracefully stop itself and clean up safely when an interrupt has been triggered. Yet, even with that PR, much more work needs to be done - more graceful interrupt logic will need to be added to Julia/Base, maybe at allocation points, the top of loops, function entry, etc. There are many possibilities, with a variety of performance and interrupt latency trade-offs, and it's not at all clear that this will come anytime soon, even if that PR is merged.

In summary: it's a hard problem, with some proposed solutions, but it will always be a battle to make graceful interrupts of arbitrary code reliable.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2474252893/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Distributed.jl/issues/105","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Distributed.jl","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Distributed.jl/issues/105/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Distributed.jl/issues/105/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Distributed.jl/issues/105/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/Distributed.jl/issues/105","id":2401793935,"node_id":"I_kwDOKdArvc6PKHeP","number":105,"title":"Failure when run inside module","user":{"login":"gbruer15","id":6753265,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY3NTMyNjU=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/6753265?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/gbruer15","html_url":"https://github.com/gbruer15","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gbruer15/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gbruer15/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gbruer15/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gbruer15/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gbruer15/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gbruer15/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gbruer15/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gbruer15/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/gbruer15/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2024-07-10T21:44:22Z","updated_at":"2024-08-02T14:06:15Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I am trying to run some parallel code defined inside a module via `include_string`, and I can't quite figure out how it works or how it's supposed to work.

It seems like a bug for such a simple case to not work, so I'm making an issue here. But it may be something that can't be fixed with Julia's current module system.

**Main question**: Is there a way to use a sandbox module without breaking Distributed.jl?

## Short failing code
A simple remote call works when run in module Main:
```julia
using Distributed
worker_ids = addprocs(2)
remotecall_wait(() -> println("I want to run this remotely"), 2)
```
But doesn't work when in a different module:
```julia
module sandbox
using Distributed
worker_ids = addprocs(2)
remotecall_wait(() -> println("I want to run this remotely"), 2)
end
```
```
ERROR: On worker 2:
UndefVarError: sandbox not defined
Stacktrace:
  [1] deserialize_module
    @ ~/.julia/juliaup/julia-1.8.5+0.x64.linux.gnu/share/julia/stdlib/v1.8/Serialization/src/Serialization.jl:996
  [2] handle_deserialize
    @ ~/.julia/juliaup/julia-1.8.5+0.x64.linux.gnu/share/julia/stdlib/v1.8/Serialization/src/Serialization.jl:895
  [3] deserialize
```

## Test scripts
I made a test script to figure out how to work with this. I run the script in a sandbox module and in the Main module.

### Driver script `mfe_driver.jl`.
```julia
filename = ARGS[1]
include_string(Module(:sandbox_mod), read(filename, String), filename)
```

###  Escape-to-main method: `mfe.jl`.
The second is a script that escapes the sandbox module to get the function to run remotely: `mfe.jl`.

<details>
  <summary>mfe.jl</summary>

```julia
using Distributed

worker_ids = addprocs(2)
try
    println("================== Main process - try to run remotely")
    try
        remotecall_wait(() -> println("I want to run this remotely"), 2)
    catch e
        showerror(stdout, e)
    end
    println()
    println()
    println("================== Main process - define and run function locally")
    function I_want_to_run_this_remotely()
        println("I want to run this remotely")
    end
    I_want_to_run_this_remotely()
    println()
    println()
    println("================== Main process - run remotely")
    try
        remotecall_wait(I_want_to_run_this_remotely, 2)
    catch e
        showerror(stdout, e)
    end
    println()
    println()
    println("================== Main process - define function in local Main and run locally")
    @everywhere [1] begin
        using Distributed
        println(" process $(myid()) has module $(@__MODULE__)")
        function I_want_to_run_this_remotely()
            println("I want to run this remotely")
        end
    end
    Main.I_want_to_run_this_remotely()
    println()
    println()
    println("================== Main process - run remotely")
    try
        remotecall_wait(Main.I_want_to_run_this_remotely, 2)
    catch e
        showerror(stdout, e)
    end
    println()
    println()
    println("================== Main process - define function in everyone's Main")
    @everywhere begin
        using Distributed
        println(" process $(myid()) has module $(@__MODULE__)")
        function I_want_to_run_this_remotely()
            println("I want to run this remotely")
        end
    end
    println()
    println()
    println("================== Main process - run remotely")
    try
        remotecall_wait(Main.I_want_to_run_this_remotely, 2)
    catch e
        showerror(stdout, e)
    end
finally
    rmprocs(worker_ids)
end
```

</details>

- In the non-sandboxed case, `remoteprocess_call` works as expected.
- The only case that fails is when the function is defined on the driver process using `@everywhere`. And I don't understand how that is different from defining the function on the driver process without `@everywhere`.

### Define-sandbox-everywhere method: `mfe_define_module.jl`

I don't want to escape the sandbox to define things in the Main scope, so I tried to work within the sandbox.

This file tries to define the sandbox module for the worker processes.

<details>
  <summary>mfe_define_module.jl</summary>

```julia
using Distributed

worker_ids = addprocs(2)
try
    println("================== Main process - determine current module information")
    current_module_local = split(string(@__MODULE__), ".")[end]
    println("  Driver module: $(@__MODULE__)")
    println("  Driver module string: $current_module_local")
    println()
    println()
    println("================== Main process - define module in workers")
    @everywhere worker_ids begin
        using Distributed
        driver_module = try
            Module(Symbol(Meta.parse($current_module_local)))
        catch e
            showerror(stdout, e)
        end
        println("Process $(myid()): ", driver_module, " ", typeof(driver_module))
    end
    println()
    println()
    driver_module = @__MODULE__
    println("================== Main process - import module in workers")
    try
        @everywhere worker_ids begin
            println("Process $(myid()): ", driver_module, " ", typeof(driver_module))
        end
    catch e
        showerror(stdout, e)
    end
    println()
    println()
    println("================== Main process - try to run remotely")
    try
        remotecall_wait(() -> println("I want to run this remotely"), 2)
    catch e
        showerror(stdout, e)
    end
    println()
    println()
    println("================== Main process - define and run function locally")
    function I_want_to_run_this_remotely()
        println("I want to run this remotely")
    end
    I_want_to_run_this_remotely()
    println()
    println()
    println("================== Main process - run remotely")
    try
        remotecall_wait(I_want_to_run_this_remotely, 2)
    catch e
        showerror(stdout, e)
    end
finally
    rmprocs(worker_ids)
end
```

</details>

- It successfully defines a module of the same name in the worker processes, but it is still not possible to call functions with `remotecall_wait`.

It seems like a bug for such a simple case to not work, so I'm making an issue here. But it may be something that can't be fixed with Julia's current module system.

**Main question**: Is there a way to use a sandbox module without breaking Distributed.jl?

## Short failing code
A simple remote call works when run in module Main:
```julia
using Distributed
worker_ids = addprocs(2)
remotecall_wait(() -> println("I want to run this remotely"), 2)
```
But doesn't work when in a different module:
```julia
module sandbox
using Distributed
worker_ids = addprocs(2)
remotecall_wait(() -> println("I want to run this remotely"), 2)
end
```
```

```

## Test scripts
I made a test script to figure out how to work with this. I run the script in a sandbox module and in the Main module.

### Driver script `mfe_driver.jl`.
```julia
filename = ARGS[1]
include_string(Module(:sandbox_mod), read(filename, String), filename)
```

###  Escape-to-main method: `mfe.jl`.
The second is a script that escapes the sandbox module to get the function to run remotely: `mfe.jl`.

<details>
  <summary>mfe.jl</summary>

```julia
using Distributed

worker_ids = addprocs(2)
try
    println("================== Main process - try to run remotely")
    try
        remotecall_wait(() -> println("I want to run this remotely"), 2)
    catch e
        showerror(stdout, e)
    end
    println()
    println()
    println("================== Main process - define and run function locally")
    function I_want_to_run_this_remotely()
        println("I want to run this remotely")
    end
    I_want_to_run_this_remotely()
    println()
    println()
    println("================== Main process - run remotely")
    try
        remotecall_wait(I_want_to_run_this_remotely, 2)
    catch e
        showerror(stdout, e)
    end
    println()
    println()
    println("================== Main process - define function in local Main and run locally")
    @everywhere [1] begin
        using Distributed
        println(" process $(myid()) has module $(@__MODULE__)")
        function I_want_to_run_this_remotely()
            println("I want to run this remotely")
        end
    end
    Main.I_want_to_run_this_remotely()
    println()
    println()
    println("================== Main process - run remotely")
    try
        remotecall_wait(Main.I_want_to_run_this_remotely, 2)
    catch e
        showerror(stdout, e)
    end
    println()
    println()
    println("================== Main process - define function in everyone's Main")
    @everywhere begin
        using Distributed
        println(" process $(myid()) has module $(@__MODULE__)")
        function I_want_to_run_this_remotely()
            println("I want to run this remotely")
        end
    end
    println()
    println()
    println("================== Main process - run remotely")
    try
        remotecall_wait(Main.I_want_to_run_this_remotely, 2)
    catch e
        showerror(stdout, e)
    end
finally
    rmprocs(worker_ids)
end
```

</details>

- In the non-sandboxed case, `remoteprocess_call` works as expected.
- The only case that fails is when the function is defined on the driver process using `@everywhere`. And I don't understand how that is different from defining the function on the driver process without `@everywhere`.

### Define-sandbox-everywhere method: `mfe_define_module.jl`

I don't want to escape the sandbox to define things in the Main scope, so I tried to work within the sandbox.

This file tries to define the sandbox module for the worker processes.

<details>
  <summary>mfe_define_module.jl</summary>

```julia
using Distributed

worker_ids = addprocs(2)
try
    println("================== Main process - determine current module information")
    current_module_local = split(string(@__MODULE__), ".")[end]
    println("  Driver module: $(@__MODULE__)")
    println("  Driver module string: $current_module_local")
    println()
    println()
    println("================== Main process - define module in workers")
    @everywhere worker_ids begin
        using Distributed
        driver_module = try
            Module(Symbol(Meta.parse($current_module_local)))
        catch e
            showerror(stdout, e)
        end
        println("Process $(myid()): ", driver_module, " ", typeof(driver_module))
    end
    println()
    println()
    driver_module = @__MODULE__
    println("================== Main process - import module in workers")
    try
        @everywhere worker_ids begin
            println("Process $(myid()): ", driver_module, " ", typeof(driver_module))
        end
    catch e
        showerror(stdout, e)
    end
    println()
    println()
    println("================== Main process - try to run remotely")
    try
        remotecall_wait(() -> println("I want to run this remotely"), 2)
    catch e
        showerror(stdout, e)
    end
    println()
    println()
    println("================== Main process - define and run function locally")
    function I_want_to_run_this_remotely()
        println("I want to run this remotely")
    end
    I_want_to_run_this_remotely()
    println()
    println()
    println("================== Main process - run remotely")
    try
        remotecall_wait(I_want_to_run_this_remotely, 2)
    catch e
        showerror(stdout, e)
    end
finally
    rmprocs(worker_ids)
end
```

</details>

- It successfully defines a module of the same name in the worker processes, but it is still not possible to call functions with `remotecall_wait`.

It seems like a bug for such a simple case to not work, so I'm making an issue here. But it may be something that can't be fixed with Julia's current module system.

**Main question**: Is there a way to use a sandbox module without breaking Distributed.jl?

## Short failing code
A simple remote call works when run in module Main:
```julia
using Distributed
worker_ids = addprocs(2)
remotecall_wait(() -> println("I want to run this remotely"), 2)
```
But doesn't work when in a different module:
```julia
module sandbox
using Distributed
worker_ids = addprocs(2)
remotecall_wait(() -> println("I want to run this remotely"), 2)
end
```
```

    @ ~/.julia/juliaup/julia-1.8.5+0.x64.linux.gnu/share/julia/stdlib/v1.8/Serialization/src/Serialization.jl:895

```

## Test scripts
I made a test script to figure out how to work with this. I run the script in a sandbox module and in the Main module.

### Driver script `mfe_driver.jl`.
```julia
filename = ARGS[1]
include_string(Module(:sandbox_mod), read(filename, String), filename)
```

###  Escape-to-main method: `mfe.jl`.
The second is a script that escapes the sandbox module to get the function to run remotely: `mfe.jl`.

<details>
  <summary>mfe.jl</summary>

```julia
using Distributed

worker_ids = addprocs(2)
try
    println("================== Main process - try to run remotely")
    try
        remotecall_wait(() -> println("I want to run this remotely"), 2)
    catch e
        showerror(stdout, e)
    end
    println()
    println()
    println("================== Main process - define and run function locally")
    function I_want_to_run_this_remotely()
        println("I want to run this remotely")
    end
    I_want_to_run_this_remotely()
    println()
    println()
    println("================== Main process - run remotely")
    try
        remotecall_wait(I_want_to_run_this_remotely, 2)
    catch e
        showerror(stdout, e)
    end
    println()
    println()
    println("================== Main process - define function in local Main and run locally")
    @everywhere [1] begin
        using Distributed
        println(" process $(myid()) has module $(@__MODULE__)")
        function I_want_to_run_this_remotely()
            println("I want to run this remotely")
        end
    end
    Main.I_want_to_run_this_remotely()
    println()
    println()
    println("================== Main process - run remotely")
    try
        remotecall_wait(Main.I_want_to_run_this_remotely, 2)
    catch e
        showerror(stdout, e)
    end
    println()
    println()
    println("================== Main process - define function in everyone's Main")
    @everywhere begin
        using Distributed
        println(" process $(myid()) has module $(@__MODULE__)")
        function I_want_to_run_this_remotely()
            println("I want to run this remotely")
        end
    end
    println()
    println()
    println("================== Main process - run remotely")
    try
        remotecall_wait(Main.I_want_to_run_this_remotely, 2)
    catch e
        showerror(stdout, e)
    end
finally
    rmprocs(worker_ids)
end
```

</details>

- In the non-sandboxed case, `remoteprocess_call` works as expected.
- The only case that fails is when the function is defined on the driver process using `@everywhere`. And I don't understand how that is different from defining the function on the driver process without `@everywhere`.

### Define-sandbox-everywhere method: `mfe_define_module.jl`

I don't want to escape the sandbox to define things in the Main scope, so I tried to work within the sandbox.

This file tries to define the sandbox module for the worker processes.

<details>
  <summary>mfe_define_module.jl</summary>

```julia
using Distributed

worker_ids = addprocs(2)
try
    println("================== Main process - determine current module information")
    current_module_local = split(string(@__MODULE__), ".")[end]
    println("  Driver module: $(@__MODULE__)")
    println("  Driver module string: $current_module_local")
    println()
    println()
    println("================== Main process - define module in workers")
    @everywhere worker_ids begin
        using Distributed
        driver_module = try
            Module(Symbol(Meta.parse($current_module_local)))
        catch e
            showerror(stdout, e)
        end
        println("Process $(myid()): ", driver_module, " ", typeof(driver_module))
    end
    println()
    println()
    driver_module = @__MODULE__
    println("================== Main process - import module in workers")
    try
        @everywhere worker_ids begin
            println("Process $(myid()): ", driver_module, " ", typeof(driver_module))
        end
    catch e
        showerror(stdout, e)
    end
    println()
    println()
    println("================== Main process - try to run remotely")
    try
        remotecall_wait(() -> println("I want to run this remotely"), 2)
    catch e
        showerror(stdout, e)
    end
    println()
    println()
    println("================== Main process - define and run function locally")
    function I_want_to_run_this_remotely()
        println("I want to run this remotely")
    end
    I_want_to_run_this_remotely()
    println()
    println()
    println("================== Main process - run remotely")
    try
        remotecall_wait(I_want_to_run_this_remotely, 2)
    catch e
        showerror(stdout, e)
    end
finally
    rmprocs(worker_ids)
end
```

</details>

- It successfully defines a module of the same name in the worker processes, but it is still not possible to call functions with `remotecall_wait`.

App support in Pkg","user":{"login":"KristofferC","id":1282691,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEyODI2OTE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1282691?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC","html_url":"https://github.com/KristofferC","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":22,"created_at":"2024-01-30T13:15:29Z","updated_at":"2024-07-28T16:45:34Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/pulls/3772","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/pull/3772","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/pull/3772.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/pull/3772.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This is quite heavily WIP towards having "app" support in Pkg. An app is a program that you just write its name in the terminal and it starts up, without explicitly having to invoke Julia, load the package, and call a function. Every app has an isolated environment.

More details of the design can be found in this hackmd: https://hackmd.io/r0sgJar5SpGNomVB8wRP_Q

This PR requires https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/52103

Here is some example usage:

```
(Pkg) pkg> app st

shell> rot13
zsh:1: command not found: rot13

(Pkg) pkg> app add https://github.com/KristofferC/Rot13.jl
    Updating git-repo `https://github.com/KristofferC/Rot13.jl`
  Activating project at `~/.julia/environments/apps/Rot13`
  No Changes to `~/.julia/environments/apps/Rot13/Project.toml`
  No Changes to `~/.julia/environments/apps/Rot13/Manifest.toml`

(@Rot13) pkg> app st
[43ef800a] Rot13 v0.1.0 `https://github.com/KristofferC/Rot13.jl#master`:  rot13 /home/kc/julia/usr/bin/julia 

shell> rot13 Rotate this please
Ebgngr
guvf
cyrnfr

(@Rot13) pkg> app rm rot13
[ Info: Deleted app rot13

(@Rot13) pkg> app st
```

cc @MasonProtter, @roger-luo

In particular, if that were our only application deployment solution, then any program which uses Dagger.jl (which contains a dynamic-dispatch based core) would not be able to be deployed as an application, and thus users would be driven away from using Dagger in their applications if it meant that they lost application support by using Dagger. That would be a pretty harmful force to have exist within our ecosystem, as dynamic dispatch serves a very useful purpose, especially when used with care to ensure it doesn't result in unnecessary slowdowns in fast-paths.

To make things more concrete, could concerned HPC developers try out this PR on their system of interest and see where any issues arise? In particular, can we identify any currently-existing pain points in the current implementation that would make it hard for application/package authors to adopt this feature in their application? That would ground the discussion around things that we can clearly identify as issues to be resolved in some way, rather than trying to throw out this idea in its entirety on the basis of known unknowns.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/comments/2254577306/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

functionality"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-05-03T10:33:22Z","updated_at":"2024-05-03T17:54:53Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Julia comes with OpenBLAS and that's our default libblastrampoline backend.  https://github.com/OpenMathLib/OpenBLAS/pull/4577 (which was a continuation of https://github.com/OpenMathLib/OpenBLAS/pull/2255 by @stevengj), which should come in the not-yet-released OpenBLAS v0.3.28, introduced callbacks to multithreading backends in OpenBLAS, which should enable composable multithreading with Julia.  I'm opening this issue for discussing if/how we want to take advantage of that, bearing in mind that by default our BLAS calls go through libblastrampoline, which is backend-agnostic, which may complicate things for writing generic code.  I think @jpsamaroo had some ideas involving `Threads.@threads`-powered callbacks or `ScopedValue`.

Also, ref #43984.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54345/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54345/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2093498896","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54345#issuecomment-2093498896","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54345","id":2093498896,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM58yEIQ","user":{"login":"jpsamaroo","id":17912843,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE3OTEyODQz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/17912843?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo","html_url":"https://github.com/jpsamaroo","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-05-03T17:54:52Z","updated_at":"2024-05-03T17:54:52Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"Regarding composability, I think the first "obvious" thing to do is to implement our callback with `Threads.@spawn` (or `Threads.@threads`, if it makes more sense) to spawn BLAS jobs, by default. Of course, we want to retain compatibility with `BLAS.set_num_threads` settings, so we can limit the number of tasks that we spawn to whatever the global setting is (or not spawn tasks at all, just directly executing jobs, when `set_num_threads(1)`).

However, one additional feature that could be quite nice is making this parallelism configurable with ScopedValues. In particular, there are utilities like `Threads.@threads` and libraries like Dagger which already provide their own multi-threaded parallelism, which would do better without BLAS being automatically multithreaded. In such cases, it would be ideal if user code (which may or may not use BLAS) could be wrapped in a `with` block that would allow disabling BLAS multithreading for operations executed within an already-multithreaded context. This could allow for overall performance gains by better utilizing known data locality and allowing libraries like Dagger to accurately track where code is executing. It should be quite straightforward to add such a mechanism using ScopedValues, and should have very minimal overhead.

Aside: How this will work for non-OpenBLAS setups, or considering libblastrampoline, is not something I have really thought about. I think we can consider the ScopedValue support to be a hint for the desired amount of internal parallelism, which would allow a graceful degradation back to the global setting when the BLAS library doesn't support this kind of mechanism.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2093498896/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"opened","number":53835,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/53835","id":1788265770,"node_id":"PR_kwDOABkWpM5qlsUq","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53835","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53835.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53835.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53835","number":53835,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"Profile: Add continuous profiling support","user":{"login":"jpsamaroo","id":17912843,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE3OTEyODQz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/17912843?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo","html_url":"https://github.com/jpsamaroo","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Users of Profile may be interested in running the profiler continuously and without stopping, such as for a background-executing runtime like Dagger.jl's. It's thus possible that we want the profiler running for longer than it would take to fill up the profile buffer. Previously, the buffer would fill up, profiling would stop, and any new traces would be lost. Additionally, sampling from the profiler with `Profile.fetch` while the profiler is running would often produce partially-written frames, requiring continuously-profiling users to clean up such frames manually.

This PR provides a new `continuous` kwarg for `Profile.init` which causes two changes in behavior when enabled:
- When the buffer becomes full, instead of stopping the Profile timer, the profiler starts recording again at the beginning of the buffer (overwriting any previously-recorded entries)
- When fetching the buffer with `Profile.fetch`, the profile lock is taken, and the entire buffer is copied out

With this feature, a user or library could use `Profile.start_timer` and periodically call `Profile.fetch` to get different samples from the profiler, all without ever stopping the profiler. Some additional support code is needed to parse entries that have been partially overwritten, but otherwise everything should work as-is.

Supersedes https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42169

Todo:
- [ ] Fix issue with `init` -> `@profile` (but not `@profile` -> `init`) causing profiling to stop working
- [ ] Trim start and end of buffer to remove invalid/empty frames
- [ ] Add tests and of Profile may be interested in running the profiler continuously and without stopping, such as for a background-executing runtime like Dagger.jl's. It's thus possible that we want the profiler running for longer than it would take to fill up the profile buffer. Previously, the buffer would fill up, profiling would stop, and any new traces would be lost. Additionally, sampling from the profiler with `Profile.fetch` while the profiler is running would often produce partially-written frames, requiring continuously-profiling users to clean up such frames manually.

This PR provides a new `continuous` kwarg for `Profile.init` which causes two changes in behavior when enabled:
- When the buffer becomes full, instead of stopping the Profile timer, the profiler starts recording again at the beginning of the buffer (overwriting any previously-recorded entries)
- When fetching the buffer with `Profile.fetch`, the profile lock is taken, and the entire buffer is copied out

With this feature, a user or library could use `Profile.start_timer` and periodically call `Profile.fetch` to get different samples from the profiler, all without ever stopping the profiler. Some additional support code is needed to parse entries that have been partially overwritten, but otherwise everything should work as-is.

Supersedes https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42169

Todo:
- [x] Fix issue with `init` -> `@profile` (but not `@profile` -> `init`) causing profiling to stop working
- [x] Trim end of buffer to remove empty frames
- [x] Add docs

buffer","user":{"login":"jpsamaroo","id":17912843,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE3OTEyODQz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/17912843?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo","html_url":"https://github.com/jpsamaroo","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":3242880567,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwzMjQyODgwNTY3","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/profiler","name":"profiler","color":"B3CE41","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2021-09-08T20:06:04Z","updated_at":"2024-03-25T19:18:44Z","closed_at":"2024-03-25T19:18:44Z","author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":true,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/42169","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42169","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42169.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42169.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"Users of Profile may be interested in running the profiler continuously and without stopping. In the case of Dagger, it's possible that multiple tasks may all want the profiler running during their execution, so we only stop the profiler if Dagger isn't running any tasks. It's thus possible that we want the profiler running for longer than it would take to fill up the profile buffer. In the old implementation, the buffer would fill up, profiling would stop, and new traces would be lost. Additionally, sampling from the profiler with `Profile.fetch` while the profiler is running would often produce partially-written frames, requiring such continuously-profiling users to clean up such frames manually.

The new implementation treats the profile buffer as a ring buffer instead. `Profile.fetch` will now start at the `len_data()` pointer offset, read until the end of the buffer, and then read the rest from the beginning to just before `len_data()`. Any zero frames at the beginning of the copied buffer are truncated, so we end up with the result we would expect without a ring buffer.

Additionally, `Profile.fetch` now takes the same locks as the profiler while copying out data from the buffer, so sampling while profiling is running should now work correctly, without broken frames.

Marking this as a draft since I still need to test this locally with In the case of Dagger, it's possible that multiple tasks may all want the profiler running during their execution, so we only stop the profiler if Dagger isn't running any tasks. It's thus possible that we want the profiler running for longer than it would take to fill up the profile buffer. In the old implementation, the buffer would fill up, profiling would stop, and new traces would be lost. Additionally, sampling from the profiler with `Profile.fetch` while the profiler is running would often produce partially-written frames, requiring such continuously-profiling users to clean up such frames manually.

The new implementation treats the profile buffer as a ring buffer instead. `Profile.fetch` will now start at the `len_data()` pointer offset, read until the end of the buffer, and then read the rest from the beginning to just before `len_data()`. Any zero frames at the beginning of the copied buffer are truncated, so we end up with the result we would expect without a ring buffer.

Additionally, `Profile.fetch` now takes the same locks as the profiler while copying out data from the buffer, so sampling while profiling is running should now work correctly, without broken frames.

Marking this as a draft since I still need to test this locally with have a usecase where I would like to remove a worker without killing it. messages.\r\n\r\nA minimal example of what I'm trying to do can be found [here](https://gist.github.com/simeonschaub/55f383445ce25fe515fd8eb5053ab3db), could adapt that into a test if so requests"},{"id":3242880567,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwzMjQyODgwNTY3","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/profiler","name":"profiler","color":"9A315A","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2023-07-19T20:16:02Z","updated_at":"2023-11-03T15:55:41Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":true,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/50604","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/50604","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/50604.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/50604.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"Very early stages, but my goal is to enable the building/loading of an external library
that provides the integration with an external timing consumer.

Currently we have three timing consumers:

1. "Timing Counts"
2. Tracy
3. ITTAPI

I have been talking to @simonbyrne and we would like to add NVTX support. @kpamnany & @nhdahly might want to implement datadog/perfetto.
Having all these implementations in-tree will not be scalable and so we should provide an interface that tools can implement
to receive runtime events/metrics.

My design criteria are:
1. Early availibity (before sysimage loading/first task creation)
2. Always on. Julia should come with timing always turned on, but reporting only happens when the user provides an actual implementation.

Right now I am reusing the `libjulia-codegen` infrastructure, but I would like to support something like

```
julia --timing-agent=libjulia-timing-tracy.so
```

or 

```
JULIA_TIMING_AGENT=libjulia-timing-tracy.so julia
```

Maybe we could support a "late initialization" for users that don't care about early startup, but might want to turn timing reporting
on and off on-demand.

cc: @topolarity & @pchintalapudi

- We want to rename this library to `libjulia-tracing` to denote the intention to support other tracing mechanisms
- We should be able to later add tracepoints to `libjulia-tracing` in a non-breaking way
- We need to have a consistent mechanism to determine whether a given `libjulia-tracing` implementation contains timing or tracing implementations (or both or neither)
- Any tracing infrastructure API and ABI should support operating at the earliest possible point during Julia initialization","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1792695862/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51657","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51657/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51657/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51657/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51657","id":1935704366,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5zYIEu","number":51657,"title":"Distributed.jl - possibility to use other Serialization libraries? ","user":{"login":"schlichtanders","id":4255480,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQyNTU0ODA=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4255480?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/schlichtanders","html_url":"https://github.com/schlichtanders","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/schlichtanders/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/schlichtanders/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/schlichtanders/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/schlichtanders/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/schlichtanders/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/schlichtanders/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/schlichtanders/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/schlichtanders/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/schlichtanders/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2023-10-10T16:00:42Z","updated_at":"2023-10-10T16:15:03Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Hi,
since recently Pluto can run Distributed, however it does not work out of the box because the standard serialization is known not to work in Pluto, but Distributed relies on it (I couldn't find a way to change this)

The PlutoSerialization is maintained in this repo https://github.com/MichaelHatherly/PlutoSerialization.jl

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51673","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51673/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51673/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51673/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51673","id":1938376788,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5ziUhU","number":51673,"title":"Thinking about exceptions","user":{"login":"BioTurboNick","id":1438610,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE0Mzg2MTA=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1438610?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/BioTurboNick","html_url":"https://github.com/BioTurboNick","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/BioTurboNick/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/BioTurboNick/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/BioTurboNick/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/BioTurboNick/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/BioTurboNick/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/BioTurboNick/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/BioTurboNick/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/BioTurboNick/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/BioTurboNick/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2023-10-11T17:50:19Z","updated_at":"2023-10-11T18:57:11Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I don't want to be so bold as to use the title of "taking ___ seriously" here, but I thought it would be useful to start a conversation about better documenting and systematizing exceptions in Julia.

I've started here by grouping most of the top-level exceptions into classes based on who should use them. Maybe comparison with exception systems in other languages, or usage in Julia or across the ecosystem, would highlight some weak spots. Is there a missing exception? Missing or incomplete docstrings? A missing constructor to provide a more useful default error message?

Exceptions:
* ArgumentError
* DimensionMismatch
* DomainError
* ErrorException
* OverflowError
* InvalidStateException

Specialized Exceptions - useful for implementing specific interfaces:
* BoundsError
* CanonicalIndexError
* KeyError
* InexactError
* StringIndexError

Runtime Exceptions - user code should never throw these:
* AssertionError
* CapturedException
* CompositeException
* ConcurrencyViolationError
* DivideError
* EOFError
* InterruptException
* MethodError
* OutOfMemoryError
* ProcessFailedException
* ReadOnlyMemoryError
* SegmentationFault
* StackOverflowError
* SystemError
* TaskFailedException
* TypeError
* UndefKeywordError
* UndefRefError
* UndefValError


Another thing I observed is that there are a few spots where a MethodError is manually thrown in Base, for instance as a fallback for unsupported `gcd` arguments:
```
gcd(pi, pi)
ERROR: MethodError: no method matching gcd(::Irrational{:π}, ::Irrational{:π})

Closest candidates are:
  gcd(::T, ::T) where T<:Real
   @ Base intfuncs.jl:136
  gcd(::Real, ::Real)
   @ Base intfuncs.jl:132
  gcd(::Real, ::Real, ::Real...)
   @ Base intfuncs.jl:134

Stacktrace:
 [1] gcd(a::Irrational{:π}, b::Irrational{:π})
   @ Base .\intfuncs.jl:136
 [2] top-level scope
   @ REPL[29]:1
```

This error doesn't make sense, because the method does exist. Perhaps instead this one could become a `DomainError`? Other instances of this and other exceptions in Base and stdlibs could be reviewed for appropriateness. Especially would be useful to identify cases where generic `error()` is being used rather than an appropriate specific exception.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51673/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51673/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1758321929","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51673#issuecomment-1758321929","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51673","id":1758321929,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5ozd0J","user":{"login":"jpsamaroo","id":17912843,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE3OTEyODQz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/17912843?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo","html_url":"https://github.com/jpsamaroo","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-10-11T18:57:11Z","updated_at":"2023-10-11T18:57:11Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"> - ConcurrencyViolationError

I use this use in my packages when certain assumptions around correct concurrent execution are violated (e.g. an object is accessed without holding the associated lock, or a concurrent state machine enters an invalid state). I personally think having this under the "Specialized Exceptions" category is more realistic.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1758321929/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

error message more accurate when adding a method to another module's function","user":{"login":"StevenWhitaker","id":18726586,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE4NzI2NTg2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/18726586?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/StevenWhitaker","html_url":"https://github.com/StevenWhitaker","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/StevenWhitaker/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/StevenWhitaker/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/StevenWhitaker/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/StevenWhitaker/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/StevenWhitaker/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/StevenWhitaker/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/StevenWhitaker/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/StevenWhitaker/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/StevenWhitaker/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2023-10-26T17:40:51Z","updated_at":"2023-10-26T19:01:15Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/51889","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/51889","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/51889.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/51889.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"I noticed the error message for adding a method to another module's function seems inaccurate.

Currently:
```julia
julia> module Mod
       f() = 1
       end
Main.Mod

julia> using .Mod: f

julia> f(x) = 2
ERROR: invalid method definition in Main: function Mod.f must be explicitly imported to be extended
Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ none:0
 [2] top-level scope
   @ REPL[3]:1
```

But I don't *have* to explicitly import `Mod.f`; I can extend it by prefixing `f` with a module path:
```julia
julia> using .Mod

julia> Mod.f(x) = 2
```

Therefore, this PR adds the clause `or prefixed with a module path` to the error message.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51889/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51889/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1781732118","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/51889#issuecomment-1781732118","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51889","id":1781732118,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5qMxMW","user":{"login":"jpsamaroo","id":17912843,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE3OTEyODQz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/17912843?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo","html_url":"https://github.com/jpsamaroo","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-10-26T19:01:14Z","updated_at":"2023-10-26T19:01:14Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"> or prefixed with a module path

I'm not sure I'd call it a "path", that feels potentially ambiguous w.r.t filesystem paths. I'd consider instead `... must be explicitly imported, or specified as Mod.f, to be extended`, since that shows the user exactly what they need to change to fix the error.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1781732118/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

functionality"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":14,"created_at":"2023-04-27T14:43:21Z","updated_at":"2023-10-31T15:43:17Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/49541","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/49541","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/49541.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/49541.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"Interrupt handling is a tricky problem, not just in terms of implementation, but in terms of desired behavior: when an interrupt is received, which code should handle it? Julia's current answer to this is effectively to throw an `InterruptException` to the first task to hit a safepoint. While this seems sensible (the code that's running gets interrupted), it only really works for very basic numerical code. In the case that multiple tasks are running concurrently, or when try-catch handlers are registered, this system breaks down, and results in unpredictable behavior. This unpredictable behavior includes:

- Interrupting background/runtime tasks which don't want to be interrupted, as they do little bits of important work (and are critical to library runtime functionality)
- Interrupting only one task, when multiple coordinating tasks would want to receive the interrupt to safely terminate a computation
- Interrupting only one library's task, when multiple libraries really would want to be notified about the interrupt

The above behavior makes it nearly impossible to provide reliable Ctrl-C behavior, and results in very confused users who get stuck hitting Ctrl-C continuously, sometimes getting caught in a hang, sometimes triggering unrelated exception handling code they didn't mean to, sometimes getting a segfault, and very rarely getting the behavior they desire (with unpredictable safety of being able to continue using the active session as intended).

This commit provides an alternative behavior for interrupts which is more predictable: user code may now register tasks as "interrupt handlers", which will be guaranteed to receive an `InterruptException` whenever the session receives an interrupt signal. Additionally, when any interrupt handlers are registered, no other tasks will receive `InterruptException`s; only the handlers may receive them.

This behavior allows one or more libraries to register handler tasks which will all be concurrently awoken to handle each interrupt and do whatever is necessary to safely interrupt any running code; the extent to which other tasks are interrupted is library-defined. For example, GPU libraries like AMDGPU.jl can register a handler to safely interrupt GPU kernels running on all GPU queues and do resource cleanup. Concurrently, a complex runtime like the scheduler in Dagger.jl can register a handler to interrupt running tasks on other workers when possible.

Todo:
- [x] ~Consider whether to allow `force` mode to still operate with handlers registered~ Not doing this for now
- [x] ~Add `@interrupthandler` API (suggested by @Seelengrab )~ Punting as we don't have a way to register the handler during `__init__`
- [x] Add a "kill foreground task" menu option
- [x] Interrupt the foreground or root task on multi-Ctrl-C in non-REPL mode
- [x] Address missing logic in `jl_task_get_next`
- [x] Add tests
- [x] Add docs","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49541/reactions","total_count":18,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":18,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49541/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1787482308","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/49541#issuecomment-1787482308","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49541","id":1787482308,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5qitDE","user":{"login":"jpsamaroo","id":17912843,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE3OTEyODQz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/17912843?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo","html_url":"https://github.com/jpsamaroo","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-10-31T15:43:17Z","updated_at":"2023-10-31T15:43:17Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"> On a related note, likely apropos #6283, I have had a need for cancellable `take!`/`put!` on channels - in the context of a Dagger-like architecture with tasks typically awaiting values from channels.
> 
> In some cases, these channels represent input from external systems (hardware), so a task (A) might be blocked on `take!` from a channel indefinitely (e.g., if no user activates the hardware button) - until e.g. another task (B) of the system decides it is time to shut down - in which case, it would be nice to be able to cancel the blocked task (A) to get graceful shutdown.
> 
> I was expecting https://github.com/davidanthoff/CancellationTokens.jl to be part of the solution (cf. [davidanthoff/CancellationTokens.jl#14](https://github.com/davidanthoff/CancellationTokens.jl/issues/14)): It might be an idea to also peek at the .NET cancellation framework.

This PR is the first necessary step towards robust cancellation - without a safe, predictable interrupt mechanism, there is no way to reliably chain interrupts to cancellation behaviors. Any attempt to implement "good" cancellation support in the language will require this PR, or something like it, to build upon.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1787482308/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

code"}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":59,"created_at":"2023-08-18T22:49:55Z","updated_at":"2023-09-05T20:08:41Z","closed_at":"2023-09-03T15:55:38Z","author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/50974","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/50974","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/50974.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/50974.patch","merged_at":"2023-09-03T15:55:38Z"},"body":"This is a bit of a straw-man proposal (though I think mergable if people agree) to standardize the execution entrypoint for Julia scripts. I think there's at least four different ways that people might run a script:

- As `julia main.jl`
- As a PkgCompiler sysimage, then calling the main entry point
- As a PkgCompiler "app", with the magic `julia_main` function
- As a StaticCompiler product with an explicit entrypoint specified on the API.

The main problem I have with all of these variants is that they're all different and it's kind of a pain to move between them. Here I propose that we standardize on `Main.main(ARGS)` as the entrypoint for all scripts. Downstream from that proposal, this PR then makes the following changes:

1. If a system image has an existing `Main.main`, that is the entry point for `julia -Jsysimage.so`.
2. If not, and the sysimage has a REPL, we call REPL.main (we could handle this by defaulting `Main.main` to a weak import of `REPL.main`, but for the purpose of this PR, it's an explicit fallback. That said, I do want to emhpasize the direction of moving the REPL to be "just another app".
3. If the REPL code is called and passed a script file, the REPL executes any newly defined Main.main after loading the script file. As a result, `julia` behaves the same as if we had generated a new system image after loading `main.jl` and then running julia with that system image.

The further downstream implication of this is that I'd like to get rid of the distinction between PkgCompiler apps and system images. An app is simply a system image with a `Main.main` function defined (note that currently PkgCompiler uses `julia_main` instead).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50974/reactions","total_count":12,"+1":10,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":2,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50974/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1707243213","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/50974#issuecomment-1707243213","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50974","id":1707243213,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5lwnbN","user":{"login":"jpsamaroo","id":17912843,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE3OTEyODQz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/17912843?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo","html_url":"https://github.com/jpsamaroo","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-09-05T20:08:40Z","updated_at":"2023-09-05T20:08:40Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"Agreed on `__main__`, and also, the proposed hacky solution sounds like it's going to hard to explain to users when it causes unexpected behavior (which it inevitably will, that's the nature of hacks). Really, `__main__` is not hard to explain to new users coming from e.g. Python, C++, or Lua, where there are also special functions that get called in semi-unexpected ways.

Let's also not forget the cost of warnings on unsuspecting users - at a minimum, it will cause churn in issue trackers and the helpdesk/Discourse/etc., but it also makes users feel like they're doing something wrong, when really they may have just wanted to use the name `main` for something not related to this feature (which IMO is totally reasonable).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1707243213/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}
