insertion","user":{"login":"Keno","id":1291671,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEyOTE2NzE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1291671?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Keno","html_url":"https://github.com/Keno","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Keno/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Keno/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Keno/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Keno/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Keno/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Keno/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Keno/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Keno/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Keno/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"This is a variant of the eager-finalization idea
(e.g. as seen in #44056), but with a focus on the mechanism
of finalizer insertion, since I need a similar pass downstream.
Integration of EscapeAnalysis is left to #44056.

My motivation for this change is somewhat different. In particular,
I want to be able to insert finalize call such that I can
subsequently SROA the mutable object. This requires a couple
design points that are more stringent than the pass from #44056,
so I decided to prototype them as an independent PR. The primary
things I need here that are not seen in #44056 are:

- The ability to forgo finalizer registration with the runtime
  entirely (requires additional legality analyis)
- The ability to inline the registered finalizer at the deallocation
  point (to enable subsequent SROA)

To this end, adding a finalizer is promoted to a builtin
that is recognized by inference and inlining (such that inference
can produce an inferred version of the finalizer for inlining).

The current status is that this fixes the minimal example I wanted
to have work, but does not yet extend to the motivating case I had.
Nevertheless, I felt that this was a good checkpoint to synchronize
with other efforts along these lines.

Currently working demo:

```julia
julia> const total_deallocations = Ref{Int}(0)
Base.RefValue{Int64}(0)

julia> mutable struct DoAlloc
               function DoAlloc()
                   this = new()
                       Core._add_finalizer(this, function(this)
                               global total_deallocations[] += 1
                       end)
                       return this
               end
       end

julia> function foo()
               for i = 1:1000
                       DoAlloc()
               end
       end
foo (generic function with 1 method)

julia> @code_llvm foo()
;  @ REPL[3]:1 within `foo`
define void @julia_foo_111() #0 {
top:
  %.promoted = load i64, i64* inttoptr (i64 140370001753968 to i64*), align 16
;  @ REPL[3]:2 within `foo`
  %0 = add i64 %.promoted, 1000
;  @ REPL[3] within `foo`
  store i64 %0, i64* inttoptr (i64 140370001753968 to i64*), align 16
;  @ REPL[3]:4 within `foo`
  ret void
}

WIP: Eager finalizer insertion","user":{"login":"Keno","id":1291671,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEyOTE2NzE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1291671?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Keno","html_url":"https://github.com/Keno","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Keno/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Keno/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Keno/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Keno/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Keno/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Keno/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Keno/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Keno/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Keno/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"This is a variant of the eager-finalization idea
(e.g. as seen in #44056), but with a focus on the mechanism
of finalizer insertion, since I need a similar pass downstream.
Integration of EscapeAnalysis is left to #44056.

My motivation for this change is somewhat different. In particular,
I want to be able to insert finalize call such that I can
subsequently SROA the mutable object. This requires a couple
design points that are more stringent than the pass from #44056,
so I decided to prototype them as an independent PR. The primary
things I need here that are not seen in #44056 are:

- The ability to forgo finalizer registration with the runtime
  entirely (requires additional legality analyis)
- The ability to inline the registered finalizer at the deallocation
  point (to enable subsequent SROA)

To this end, adding a finalizer is promoted to a builtin
that is recognized by inference and inlining (such that inference
can produce an inferred version of the finalizer for inlining).

The current status is that this fixes the minimal example I wanted
to have work, but does not yet extend to the motivating case I had.
Nevertheless, I felt that this was a good checkpoint to synchronize
with other efforts along these lines.

Currently working demo:

```julia
julia> const total_deallocations = Ref{Int}(0)
Base.RefValue{Int64}(0)

julia> mutable struct DoAlloc
               function DoAlloc()
                   this = new()
                       Core._add_finalizer(this, function(this)
                               global total_deallocations[] += 1
                       end)
                       return this
               end
       end

julia> function foo()
               for i = 1:1000
                       DoAlloc()
               end
       end
foo (generic function with 1 method)

julia> @code_llvm foo()
;  @ REPL[3]:1 within `foo`
define void @julia_foo_111() #0 {
top:
  %.promoted = load i64, i64* inttoptr (i64 140370001753968 to i64*), align 16
;  @ REPL[3]:2 within `foo`
  %0 = add i64 %.promoted, 1000
;  @ REPL[3] within `foo`
  store i64 %0, i64* inttoptr (i64 140370001753968 to i64*), align 16
;  @ REPL[3]:4 within `foo`
  ret void
}
```

insertion","user":{"login":"Keno","id":1291671,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEyOTE2NzE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1291671?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Keno","html_url":"https://github.com/Keno","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Keno/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Keno/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Keno/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Keno/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Keno/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Keno/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Keno/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Keno/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Keno/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2022-05-11T07:13:22Z","updated_at":"2022-05-11T16:17:02Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/45272","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/45272","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/45272.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/45272.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This is a variant of the eager-finalization idea
(e.g. as seen in #44056), but with a focus on the mechanism
of finalizer insertion, since I need a similar pass downstream.
Integration of EscapeAnalysis is left to #44056.

My motivation for this change is somewhat different. In particular,
I want to be able to insert finalize call such that I can
subsequently SROA the mutable object. This requires a couple
design points that are more stringent than the pass from #44056,
so I decided to prototype them as an independent PR. The primary
things I need here that are not seen in #44056 are:

- The ability to forgo finalizer registration with the runtime
  entirely (requires additional legality analyis)
- The ability to inline the registered finalizer at the deallocation
  point (to enable subsequent SROA)

To this end, adding a finalizer is promoted to a builtin
that is recognized by inference and inlining (such that inference
can produce an inferred version of the finalizer for inlining).

The current status is that this fixes the minimal example I wanted
to have work, but does not yet extend to the motivating case I had.
Nevertheless, I felt that this was a good checkpoint to synchronize
with other efforts along these lines.

Currently working demo:

```julia
julia> const total_deallocations = Ref{Int}(0)
Base.RefValue{Int64}(0)

julia> mutable struct DoAlloc
               function DoAlloc()
                   this = new()
                       Core._add_finalizer(this, function(this)
                               global total_deallocations[] += 1
                       end)
                       return this
               end
       end

julia> function foo()
               for i = 1:1000
                       DoAlloc()
               end
       end
foo (generic function with 1 method)

julia> @code_llvm foo()
;  @ REPL[3]:1 within `foo`
define void @julia_foo_111() #0 {
top:
  %.promoted = load i64, i64* inttoptr (i64 140370001753968 to i64*), align 16
;  @ REPL[3]:2 within `foo`
  %0 = add i64 %.promoted, 1000
;  @ REPL[3] within `foo`
  store i64 %0, i64* inttoptr (i64 140370001753968 to i64*), align 16
;  @ REPL[3]:4 within `foo`
  ret void
}
```

methods","user":{"login":"nlw0","id":161042,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE2MTA0Mg==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/161042?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/nlw0","html_url":"https://github.com/nlw0","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nlw0/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nlw0/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nlw0/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nlw0/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nlw0/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nlw0/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nlw0/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nlw0/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nlw0/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2022-04-04T17:51:11Z","updated_at":"2022-04-04T22:41:17Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/44851","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44851","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44851.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44851.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This implements a `depth` feature for `walkdir`, similar to #43039

I made this PR a bit as an exercise, I would love to hear comments about the proposal, and if there's any interest in pursuing this.

The code has been refactored so that there are two separate steps, first a tree structure is produced, and then traversed. The tree traversal step handles the order and depth arguments, while the first step handles `follow_symlinks`.

This code is missing the error handling features, that seem harder to implement. Could there be an alternative way to perform this error handling, like moving the try statement to a higher level?

This function also relates to #44845.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44851/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44851/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1088082133","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44851#issuecomment-1088082133","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44851","id":1088082133,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5A2tDV","user":{"login":"jpsamaroo","id":17912843,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE3OTEyODQz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/17912843?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo","html_url":"https://github.com/jpsamaroo","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-04-04T22:41:17Z","updated_at":"2022-04-04T22:41:17Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"Maybe this logic can be enabled via a kwarg, like `eager=true`?      threads use [`addprocs`](@ref) and pass `-t`/`--threads` as `exeflags`.
 
-## Data-race freedom
+## [Threadpools](@id man-threadpools)
+
+When a program's threads are busy with many tasks to run, tasks may experience
+delays which may negatively affect the responsiveness and interactivity of the
+program. To address this, you can specify that a task is interactive when you
+[`Threads.@spawn`](@ref) it. Julia may be started with one or more threads
+reserved to run interactive tasks:
+
+```bash
+$ julia --threads 3,1
+```","path":"doc/src/manual/multi-threading.md","position":null,"original_position":15,"commit_id":"55aef9f204cba976442ed069f7245cd8d8896fbb","original_commit_id":"1069a7adf5d0331718f63c76b73d30f130c87084","user":{"login":"jpsamaroo","id":17912843,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE3OTEyODQz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/17912843?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo","html_url":"https://github.com/jpsamaroo","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"I      three processes have 2 threads enabled. For more fine grained control over worker
     threads use [`addprocs`](@ref) and pass `-t`/`--threads` as `exeflags`.
 
-## Data-race freedom
+## [Threadpools](@id man-threadpools)
+
+When a program's threads are busy with many tasks to run, tasks may experience
+delays which may negatively affect the responsiveness and interactivity of the
+program. To address this, you can specify that a task is interactive when you
+[`Threads.@spawn`](@ref) it. Julia may be started with one or more threads
+reserved to run interactive tasks:
+
+```bash
+$ julia --threads 3,1
+```","path":"doc/src/manual/multi-threading.md","position":null,"original_position":15,"commit_id":"55aef9f204cba976442ed069f7245cd8d8896fbb","original_commit_id":"1069a7adf5d0331718f63c76b73d30f130c87084","user":{"login":"jpsamaroo","id":17912843,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE3OTEyODQz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/17912843?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo","html_url":"https://github.com/jpsamaroo","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"I think it's reasonable to expect users to opt-in to having an extra thread for now (yes, an *extra* thread, not trading one for another). I think we also should keep the default threadpool at `Sys.CPU_THREADS`, and just have 1 additional thread for interactive uses, if opted-into.

My understanding is that I/O is typically looked favorably upon by OS schedulers, and will cause high-I/O threads to get more compute time versus a thread which rarely does I/O. This should result in the interactive thread getting execution time when it needs it, but as long as the interactive work is truly low-latency and "sparse", it shouldn't significantly impact the default threadpool's performance. So adding an extra thread shouldn't significantly bog down performance sensistive, high-latency base/compiler/ssair/)"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":6,"created_at":"2022-02-06T22:23:03Z","updated_at":"2022-03-08T03:09:04Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/44056","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44056","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44056.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44056.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"Finalizers are a great way to defer the freeing side of memory management until some later point; however, they can have unpredictable behavior when the data that they free is not fully known to the GC (e.g. GPU allocations, or distributed references). This can result in behavior like out-of-memory situations, excessive memory usage, and sometimes more costly freeing behavior (in the event that locks need to be taken).

This seems like a bad situation, but there is a silver lining: some code patterns which allocate such objects don't actually need the allocations to stick around very long, and the lifetime of the object could (in theory) be statically determined by the compiler. Thankfully, with the ongoing work of integrating EscapeAnalysis.jl into the optimizer, we can use the generated escape information to improve this situation.

This PR uses escape info from EA to determine when an object has an attached finalizer, and when its lifetime is provably finite (i.e. the object does not escape the analyzed scope). For such objects, we can insert an early call to `finalize(obj)` at the end of `obj`'s lifetime, which will allow the object's finalizer to be enqueued for execution immediately, minimizing how long finalizeable object stay live in the GC.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44056/reactions","total_count":18,"+1":7,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":11,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44056/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1061360328","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44056#issuecomment-1061360328","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44056","id":1061360328,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM4_QxLI","user":{"login":"jpsamaroo","id":17912843,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE3OTEyODQz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/17912843?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo","html_url":"https://github.com/jpsamaroo","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-03-08T03:09:04Z","updated_at":"2022-03-08T03:09:04Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"@yuyichao while I do agree with that being the long-term goal for many usages, the counter-argument against directly calling the finalizer thunk is that users may not have written their finalizers such that they can be safely called in the same scope as the allocation (for example, if the finalizer takes a non-reentrant lock that is already held in the allocation scope). For now, the goal is to let the GC know (through any means) as soon as possible that the allocation is dead and that its finalizers may run.

> you can simply add a new C API to pass in the finalizer directly so that you can skip the scan of the finalizer list and have that C API set appropriate flags for the GC.

I agree with adding this fast-path; would it be reasonable to punt that to a future PR, or do you want to see that done here before this is considered for merge? I would like to avoid sinking too much time on the extra complexity right now, since (IMO) the core of this PR is simple and easily understood.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1061360328/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

base/compiler/ssair/)"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":7,"created_at":"2022-02-06T22:23:03Z","updated_at":"2022-03-08T03:17:38Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/44056","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44056","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44056.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44056.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"Finalizers are a great way to defer the freeing side of memory management until some later point; however, they can have unpredictable behavior when the data that they free is not fully known to the GC (e.g. GPU allocations, or distributed references). This can result in behavior like out-of-memory situations, excessive memory usage, and sometimes more costly freeing behavior (in the event that locks need to be taken).

This seems like a bad situation, but there is a silver lining: some code patterns which allocate such objects don't actually need the allocations to stick around very long, and the lifetime of the object could (in theory) be statically determined by the compiler. Thankfully, with the ongoing work of integrating EscapeAnalysis.jl into the optimizer, we can use the generated escape information to improve this situation.

This PR uses escape info from EA to determine when an object has an attached finalizer, and when its lifetime is provably finite (i.e. the object does not escape the analyzed scope). For such objects, we can insert an early call to `finalize(obj)` at the end of `obj`'s lifetime, which will allow the object's finalizer to be enqueued for execution immediately, minimizing how long finalizeable object stay live in the GC.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44056/reactions","total_count":18,"+1":7,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":11,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44056/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1061364092","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44056#issuecomment-1061364092","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44056","id":1061364092,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM4_QyF8","user":{"login":"jpsamaroo","id":17912843,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE3OTEyODQz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/17912843?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo","html_url":"https://github.com/jpsamaroo","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-03-08T03:17:38Z","updated_at":"2022-03-08T03:17:38Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"I've gotten this working on basic functions, and added support for inserting `finalize` before `throw`:

```julia
function g(cond, x)
  r = Ref{Any}()
  finalizer(r) do _r
  end
  r[] = x
  if cond
    error("Lol")
    return r[]
  end
  return r
end
```

initially looks like

```
2  1 ─ %1 = %new(Base.RefValue{Any})::Base.RefValue{Any}                                                                                                      │╻╷ Ref
3  │   %2 = %new(Main.:(var"#13#14"))::Core.Const(var"#13#14"())                                                                                              │
   │   %3 = $(Expr(:foreigncall, :(:jl_get_ptls_states), Ptr{Nothing}, svec(), 0, :(:ccall)))::Ptr{Nothing}                                                   │╻  finalizer
   │        $(Expr(:foreigncall, :(:jl_gc_add_finalizer_th), Nothing, svec(Ptr{Nothing}, Any, Any), 0, :(:ccall), :(%3), :(%1), :(%2), :(%3)))::Nothing       ││
5  │        Base.setfield!(%1, :x, _3)::String                                                                                                                │╻╷ setindex!
6  └──      goto #3 if not _2                                                                                                                                 │
7  2 ─      invoke Main.error("Lol"::String)::Union{}                                                                                                         │
   └──      unreachable                                                                                                                                       │
10 3 ─      return %1
```

and with this pass, becomes

```
2  1 ─ %1  = %new(Base.RefValue{Any})::Base.RefValue{Any}                                                                                                     │╻╷ Ref
3  │   %2  = %new(Main.:(var"#13#14"))::Core.Const(var"#13#14"())                                                                                             │
   │   %3  = $(Expr(:foreigncall, :(:jl_get_ptls_states), Ptr{Nothing}, svec(), 0, :(:ccall)))::Ptr{Nothing}                                                  │╻  finalizer
   │         $(Expr(:foreigncall, :(:jl_gc_add_finalizer_th), Nothing, svec(Ptr{Nothing}, Any, Any), 0, :(:ccall), :(%3), :(%1), :(%2), :(%3)))::Nothing      ││
5  │         Base.setfield!(%1, :x, _3)::String                                                                                                               │╻╷ setindex!
6  └──       goto #3 if not _2                                                                                                                                │
7  2 ─ %10 = $(Expr(:foreigncall, :(:jl_get_current_task), Ref{Task}, svec(), 0, :(:ccall)))::Ref{Task}                                                       │
   │         $(Expr(:foreigncall, :(:jl_finalize_th), Nothing, svec(Any, Any), 0, :(:ccall), :(%10), :(%1)))::Nothing
   │         invoke Main.error("Lol"::String)::Union{}
   └──       unreachable                                                                                                                                      │
10 3 ─       return %1
```

I've updated the core logic with some debugging statements to help me figure out why this isn't working in certain cases (specifically, we're failing bootstrap right now); they will be removed once the code is functioning correctly.

If anyone has ideas for what I'm doing wrong, I would appreciate any feedback :slightly_smiling_face: ","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1061364092/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Are packages currently doing such resource handling in finalizers, given we don't have https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/35689?

Yes, MemPool.jl is using a global non-reentrant lock (taken from CUDAdrv/CUDAnative originally), which is taken during allocation, and during finalization. I wouldn't mind changing it to a regular `ReentrantLock` if this is considered to be an unsupported pattern (I'm not sure if it really needs to be non-reentrant anymore, now that we disable finalizers while taking locks; @krynju).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1061889257/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

go faster"},{"id":9159719,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw5MTU5NzE5","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/parallel","name":"parallel","color":"f7e102","default":false,"description":"Parallel or distributed computation"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2022-03-18T16:42:50Z","updated_at":"2022-03-18T19:28:36Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/44671","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44671","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44671.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44671.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"It seems like `@sync_add` adds the `Futures` to a queue (`Channel`) for `@sync`, which in turn calls `wait()` for all the futures synchronously and serially. Not only that is slightly detrimental for network operations (latencies add up), but in case of Distributed the call to wait() may actually cause some compilation on remote processes, which is also wait()ed for. In result, some operations took a great amount of "serial" processing time if executed on many workers at once.

For me, this closes #44645.

The major change can be illustrated as follows:

First add some workers:

```
using Distributed
addprocs(10)
```

and then trigger something that, for example, causes package imports on the workers:

```
using SomeTinyPackage
```

In my case (importing UnicodePlots on 10 workers), this improves the loading time over 10 workers from ~11s to ~5.5s.

This is a far bigger issue when worker count gets high. The time of the processing on each worker is usually around 0.3s, so triggering this problem even on a relatively small cluster (64 workers) causes a really annoying delay, and running `@everywhere` for the first time on reasonable clusters (I tested with 1024 workers, see #44645) usually takes more than 5 minutes. Which sucks.

Anyway, on 64 workers this reduces the "first import" time from ~30s to ~6s, and on 1024 workers this seems to reduce the time from over 5 minutes (I didn't bother to measure that precisely now, sorry) to ~11s.

Related issues:
- Probably fixes #39291.
- #42156 is a kinda complementary -- it removes the most painful source of   slowness (the 0.3s precompilation on the workers), but the fact that the wait()ing is serial remains a problem if the network latencies are docstring"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":33,"created_at":"2020-05-11T00:27:41Z","updated_at":"2022-03-31T17:46:47Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/35833","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/35833","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/35833.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/35833.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This PR proposes *context variables* API that can be used to propagate context-dependent information across task boundaries.  It is conceptually similar to `task_local_storage` but with a main difference that it "copies" all key-value pairs to child `Task`s.  The context variables are conceptually similar to dynamically scoped variables.

## Motivations

There are several places context variables can be useful or required.

### `ENV`

It was proposed to use `task_local_storage` to fix thread-safety of `withenv` https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/34726#issuecomment-586138687 by maintaining task-local copy (or overlay) of `ENV`.  However, it would mean that the environment variables cannot cross task boundaries.  Context variable can fix this shortcoming.

### `@testset`

`@testset` uses `task_local_storage` to track current active test set.  However, following example does not work (prints `No tests`) because the information of the current test does not propagate to the child task:

```julia
@testset begin
    @sync @async @test true
end
```

### Logging

`Task` has the `logstate` field that propagates to child tasks; i.e., it works as a hard-coded context variable.  Once context variable handling is sufficiently matured, it may be possible to eliminate the special handling from `Task` and use a context variable for `Task`.  Furthermore, context variable allows users to develop logging-like interfaces.

### Custom worker pool abstraction

In #35757 and [Propagation of available/assigned worker-IDs in hierarchical computations? - Domains / Julia at Scale - JuliaLang](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/propagation-of-available-assigned-worker-ids-in-hierarchical-computations/38670), it was brought up that propagating "computation resources" (thread/process pools, etc.) across tasks and processes is required for implementing custom worker pool interfaces.

### Misc.

> Request-related data, such as security tokens and request data in web applications, language context for gettext, etc.
>
> --- [Rationale section in PEP 0567](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0567/#rationale)

It would be useful for implementing a better nestable progress information handling https://github.com/JunoLab/ProgressLogging.jl/pull/13#discussion_r371057967.

## Other languages

### Implicit context

* Python has [`contextvars`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/contextvars.html). See [PEP 567](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0567/) and [PEP 550](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0567/) for discussion.
* .NET has [`ExecutionContext`](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/api/system.threading.executioncontext?view=netcore-3.1).
* Kotlin has [`CoroutineContext`](https://kotlinlang.org/docs/reference/coroutines/coroutine-context-and-dispatchers.html).

### Explicit context

* Go has [`context`](https://blog.golang.org/context).

## Proposed design

I propose an API with the following basic usages.

* Declare context variable: `@contextvar x`
* Get the value: `x[]`
* Set the value: `x[] = value`

For a tutorial and the full reference API of the proposed design, see https://tkf.github.io/ContextVariablesX.jl/dev/

Internally, `@contextvar` creates an instance of `ContextVar` which is defined as

```julia
struct ContextVar{T}
    key::UUID
    default::T
    ...
end
```

Then `x[]` and `x[] = value` invoke `current_task().ctxvars[x.key]` and an "immutable version" of `current_task().ctxvars[x.key] = value`, respectively (roughly speaking).

This has a couple of nice properties:

1. `x[]` can be inferred
2. `x` is forced to be namespaced (i.e., it has to exist in some module name space.)
3. `x` can be backed up by an efficient concrete key type (e.g., UUID)
4. `x` allows small-size optimization when the value type can be inlined into the context storage (in principle)

See also https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/35757 which already contains some discussion on this API.

makes it possible to track when and on what task an allocation occurred, similar to what we have for the time-based profiler.

base/compiler/ssair/)"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2022-02-06T22:23:03Z","updated_at":"2022-02-09T15:16:15Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":true,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/44056","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44056","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44056.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44056.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"Finalizers are a great way to defer the freeing side of memory management until some later point; however, they can have unpredictable behavior when the data that they free is not fully known to the GC (e.g. GPU allocations, or distributed references). This can result in behavior like out-of-memory situations, excessive memory usage, and sometimes more costly freeing behavior (in the event that locks need to be taken).

This seems like a bad situation, but there is a silver lining: some code patterns which allocate such objects don't actually need the allocations to stick around very long, and the lifetime of the object could (in theory) be statically determined by the compiler. Thankfully, with the ongoing work of integrating EscapeAnalysis.jl into the optimizer, we can use the generated escape information to improve this situation.

This PR uses escape info from EA to determine when an object has an attached finalizer, and when its lifetime is provably finite (i.e. the object does not escape the analyzed scope). For such objects, we can insert an early call to `finalize(obj)` at the end of `obj`'s lifetime, which will allow the object's finalizer to be enqueued for execution immediately, minimizing how long finalizeable object stay live in the GC.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44056/reactions","total_count":13,"+1":5,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":8,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44056/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1033869956","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44056#issuecomment-1033869956","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44056","id":1033869956,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM49n5qE","user":{"login":"jpsamaroo","id":17912843,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE3OTEyODQz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/17912843?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo","html_url":"https://github.com/jpsamaroo","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-02-09T15:16:15Z","updated_at":"2022-02-09T15:16:15Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"Per required"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":42,"created_at":"2021-09-18T18:42:07Z","updated_at":"2022-02-23T18:54:09Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/42302","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42302","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42302.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42302.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This PR implements threadpool support in the runtime, allowing extra threadpools of arbitrary sizes to be created at startup. The existing set of threads (specified by `-t` or `JULIA_NUM_THREADS`) are placed in threadpool 1, and subsequent threadpools (specified by `-T`, `--threadpools`, or `JULIA_THREADPOOLS` as a comma-separated list of threadpool sizes) get their own increasing integer ID. The approach I'm working towards is having threadpools act independently of each other, having dedicated PARTR queues, so that tasks placed in one threadpool do not migrate into other threadpools without user intervention.

I've tried to make the changes as non-breaking as possible, although it's definitely possible that users are relying on `Threads.threadid()` and `Threads.nthreads()` to be absolute w.r.t the total number of threads.

Todo:
- [x] Fix task placement via `jl_set_task_tid` into non-default threadpool
- [x] Implement multiq threadpool isolation in PARTR
- [x] Validate GC works on extra threadpools
- [x] Implement control layer for assigning library tasks to threadpools
- [x] Add (optional?) user-defined names to threadpools
- [x] Switch API to `@enum` threadpool "classes", and define a few classes
- [x] Add support to `Threads.@spawn` for annotating class (`Threads.@spawn Threads.LowLatency ...`)
- [ ] Add docs
- [ ] Add tests with extra threadpool
- [ ] Fix `Threads.@spawn` spawning back into the default threadpool
- [ ] `Threadpools.assign!` should not spawn into the first thread in a threadpool
- [ ] Test out absolute thread IDs as main API
- [x] Remove `Threads.resize_nthreads!` (ref https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42309)

@vchuravy @vtjnash @tkf  I'd appreciate your reviews of this proposed approach and  0x0000000000000000 (NULL)               0x0

 3. `julia:gc__mark__begin`: Beginning the mark phase
 4. `julia:gc__mark_end(scanned_bytes, perm_scanned)`: Mark phase ended
 5. `julia:gc__sweep_begin(full)`: Starting sweep
-6. `julia:gc__sweep_end()`: Sweep phase finished
+6. `julia:gc__sweep_end`: Sweep phase finished
 7. `julia:gc__end`: GC is finished, other threads continue work
 8. `julia:gc__finalizer`: Initial GC thread has finished running finalizers
 
+### Task runtime probes
+
+1. `julia:rt__run__task(task, ptls)`: Switching to task `task` on thread associated with PTLS `ptls`.
+2. `julia:rt__pause__task(task, ptls)`: Switching from task `task` on thread associated with PTLS `ptls`.
+3. `julia:rt__new__task(parent, child, ptls)`: Task `parent` created task `child` on thread associated with PTLS `ptls`.
+4. `julia:rt__start__process__events(task)`: Task `task` started processing libuv events.
+5. `julia:rt__finish__process__events(task)`: Task `task` finished processing libuv events.
+6. `julia:rt__multiq__insert__success(ptls)`: Thread with PTLS `ptls` successfully inserted a task.
+7. `julia:rt__multiq__insert__full(ptls)`: Thread with PTLS `ptls` failed to insert a task because the multiq was full.
+8. `julia:rt__multiq__deletemin__trylock__success(ptls, heap)`: Thread with PTLS `ptls` successfully acquired the lock for multiq heap `heap`.
+9. `julia:rt__multiq__deletemin__trylock__failed(ptls, heap)`: Thread with PTLS `ptls` failed to acquire a lock. If the number of failed lock attempts significantly exceeds the number of successful locks, it indicates the contention on the (re)scheduling of non-sticky tasks.
+10. `julia:rt__multiq__deletemin__success(ptls, task)`: Thread with PTLS `ptls` successfully acquired task `task` from the multiq.

functionality"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":37,"created_at":"2021-09-18T18:42:07Z","updated_at":"2022-01-05T16:30:47Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":true,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/42302","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42302","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42302.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42302.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This PR implements threadpool support in the runtime, allowing extra threadpools of arbitrary sizes to be created at runtime. The existing set of threads (specified by `-t` or `JULIA_NUM_THREADS`) are placed in threadpool 1, and subsequent threadpools get their own increasing integer ID. The approach I'm working towards is having threadpools act independently of each other, having dedicated PARTR queues, so that tasks placed in one threadpool do not migrate into other threadpools without user intervention.

I've tried to make the changes as non-breaking as possible, although it's definitely possible that users are relying on `Threads.threadid()` and `Threads.nthreads()` to be absolute w.r.t the total number of threads.

Todo:
- [ ] Fix task placement via `jl_set_task_tid` into non-default threadpool
- [ ] Implement dedicated multiq support in PARTR
- [ ] Validate GC works on extra threadpools
- [ ] Add docs
- [ ] Add tests with extra threadpool
- [x] Remove `Threads.resize_nthreads!` (ref https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42309)

- The threadpool concept is probably necessary for the future of interactive Julia libraries, like Gtk.jl
- `nthreads()` usage is a problem with this PR (as we all know), but it's probably something that we'll need to deal with no matter what do.
- Separate threadpool scheduling may provide the necessary layer of separation required for https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43649
- Threadpools (if we stick with this mechanism) should be statically sized at startup, and *not* dynamically created/resized at runtime
- To prevent the loss of composability from libraries using this feature, we can implement an orchestration/control layer that is user/application-controlled, putting the responsibility of mapping threadpools to sets of tasks at the highest level of control. By also statically sizing threadpools, we prevent libraries from conflicting with each other by creating too many isolated threadpools.

So now the question is, what should that control layer look like? How do we enable the user to expose a threadpool to a library? Do we use strings to identify a threadpool (like "main", or "lowlat", or "interactive", etc.).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1005880262/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

-6. `julia:gc__sweep_end()`: Sweep phase finished
+6. `julia:gc__sweep_end`: Sweep phase finished

+### Task runtime probes
+
+1. `julia:rt__run__task(task, ptls)`: Switching to task `task` on thread associated with PTLS `ptls`.
+2. `julia:rt__pause__task(task, ptls)`: Switching from task `task` on thread associated with PTLS `ptls`.
+3. `julia:rt__new__task(parent, child, ptls)`: Task `parent` created task `child` on thread associated with PTLS `ptls`.
+4. `julia:rt__start__process__events(task)`: Task `task` started processing libuv events.
+5. `julia:rt__finish__process__events(task)`: Task `task` finished processing libuv events.
+6. `julia:rt__multiq__insert__success(ptls)`: Thread with PTLS `ptls` successfully inserted a task.
+7. `julia:rt__multiq__insert__full(ptls)`: Thread with PTLS `ptls` failed to insert a task because the multiq was full.
+8. `julia:rt__multiq__deletemin__trylock__success(ptls, heap)`: Thread with PTLS `ptls` successfully acquired the lock for multiq heap `heap`.
+9. `julia:rt__multiq__deletemin__trylock__failed(ptls, heap)`: Thread with PTLS `ptls` failed to acquire a lock. If the number of failed lock attempts significantly exceeds the number of successful locks, it indicates the contention on the (re)scheduling of non-sticky tasks.
+10. `julia:rt__multiq__deletemin__success(ptls, task)`: Thread with PTLS `ptls` successfully acquired task `task` from the multiq.
+11. `julia:rt__multiq__deletemin__emptyish(ptls, task)`: Thread with PTLS `ptls` failed to acquire a task due to the multiq being approximately functionality"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":38,"created_at":"2021-09-18T18:42:07Z","updated_at":"2022-01-11T17:28:20Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":true,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/42302","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42302","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42302.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42302.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This PR implements threadpool support in the runtime, allowing extra threadpools of arbitrary sizes to be created at startup. The existing set of threads (specified by `-t` or `JULIA_NUM_THREADS`) are placed in threadpool 1, and subsequent threadpools (specified by `-T`, `--threadpools`, or `JULIA_THREADPOOLS` as a comma-separated list of threadpool sizes) get their own increasing integer ID. The approach I'm working towards is having threadpools act independently of each other, having dedicated PARTR queues, so that tasks placed in one threadpool do not migrate into other threadpools without user intervention.

I've tried to make the changes as non-breaking as possible, although it's definitely possible that users are relying on `Threads.threadid()` and `Threads.nthreads()` to be absolute w.r.t the total number of threads.

Todo:
- [x] Fix task placement via `jl_set_task_tid` into non-default threadpool
- [ ] Implement multiq threadpool isolation in PARTR
- [x] Validate GC works on extra threadpools
- [ ] Implement control layer for assigning library tasks to threadpools
- [ ] Add docs
- [ ] Add tests with extra threadpool
- [x] Remove `Threads.resize_nthreads!` (ref https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42309)

@vchuravy @vtjnash @tkf  I'd appreciate your reviews of this proposed approach and implementation","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/42302/reactions","total_count":6,"+1":3,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":3,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/42302/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1010194293","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42302#issuecomment-1010194293","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/42302","id":1010194293,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM48Nld1","user":{"login":"jpsamaroo","id":17912843,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE3OTEyODQz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/17912843?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo","html_url":"https://github.com/jpsamaroo","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-01-11T17:28:20Z","updated_at":"2022-01-11T17:28:20Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"Initial thoughts on the control layer:
- Built-in to Base/Threads (`Threads.Control`?)
- Threadpools must be identified by a string, with the primary threadpool having the name "main"
- The user can call `Threads.Control.set_assignment!(pool::String, name::String)` to setup a global mapping from `name` to the threadpool `pool`. Libraries may then call `Threads.Control.assign!(t::Task, name::String)`; if a mapping exists for `name`, will assign `t` to the specified threadpool. If a mapping does not exist, it will default to assigning `t` to the main threadpool. The task `t` must be currently unscheduled.
- Libraries are generally discouraged from querying the mappings in `Threads.Control`, except for display purposes.
- Libraries are *encouraged* to query the sizes of threadpools (which will also be in `Threads.Control`) for static sizing of global data structures (until we have a better alternative).
- Libraries should only use `Threads.Control.set_assignment!` based on a user decision (such as providing a "pretty" UI for threadpool assignments).
- Libraries can assume that `Threads.nthreads` is constant within a task after they've been assigned (for now), as tasks may assume that they will never be moved to a different threadpool. Migrating an unassuming task between threadpools after the task has started is undefined behavior (mostly because I haven't tested it).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1010194293/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

-# Inclusive upper bound on threadid()

-    Threads.nthreads()
+    Threads.threadid() -> Int

+Get the ID number of the current thread of execution within the Julia session.
+The master thread has ID `1`.
+"""
+rawthreadid() = Int(ccall(:jl_threadid, Int16, ())+1)
+
+"""
+    Threads.nthreads(tp::Int=Threads.threadpoolid()) -> Int
+
+Get the number of threads available in the specified threadpool. This is the
+inclusive upper bound on [`threadid()`](@ref).
 
 See also: `BLAS.get_num_threads` and `BLAS.set_num_threads` in the

 [`Distributed`](@ref man-distributed) standard library.

+nthreads(tp::Int=Threads.threadpoolid()) =

+

+    Threads.spawn_threadpool(n::Int) -> Int
+
+Spawns a new threadpool of size `n`, and returns the threadpool ID.
+"""
+function spawn_threadpool(n::Int)
+    wq = [Base.StickyWorkqueue() for _ in 1:n]

PR implements threadpool support in the runtime, allowing extra threadpools of arbitrary sizes to be created at startup. The existing set of threads (specified by `-t` or `JULIA_NUM_THREADS`) are placed in threadpool 1, and subsequent threadpools (specified by `-T`, `--threadpools`, or `JULIA_THREADPOOLS` as a comma-separated list of threadpool sizes) get their own increasing integer ID. The approach I'm working towards is having threadpools act independently of each other, having dedicated PARTR queues, so that tasks placed in one threadpool do not migrate into other threadpools without user intervention.

I've tried to make the changes as non-breaking as possible, although it's definitely possible that users are relying on `Threads.threadid()` and `Threads.nthreads()` to be absolute w.r.t the total number of threads.

Todo:
- [x] Fix task placement via `jl_set_task_tid` into non-default threadpool
- [x] Implement multiq threadpool isolation in PARTR
- [x] Validate GC works on extra threadpools
- [x] Implement control layer for assigning library tasks to threadpools
- [ ] Add (optional?) user-defined names to threadpools
- [ ] Add docs
- [ ] Add tests with extra threadpool
- [x] Remove `Threads.resize_nthreads!` (ref https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42309)

PR implements threadpool support in the runtime, allowing extra threadpools of arbitrary sizes to be created at startup. The existing set of threads (specified by `-t` or `JULIA_NUM_THREADS`) are placed in threadpool 1, and subsequent threadpools (specified by `-T`, `--threadpools`, or `JULIA_THREADPOOLS` as a comma-separated list of threadpool sizes) get their own increasing integer ID. The approach I'm working towards is having threadpools act independently of each other, having dedicated PARTR queues, so that tasks placed in one threadpool do not migrate into other threadpools without user intervention.

I've tried to make the changes as non-breaking as possible, although it's definitely possible that users are relying on `Threads.threadid()` and `Threads.nthreads()` to be absolute w.r.t the total number of threads.

Todo:
- [x] Fix task placement via `jl_set_task_tid` into non-default threadpool
- [x] Implement multiq threadpool isolation in PARTR
- [x] Validate GC works on extra threadpools
- [x] Implement control layer for assigning library tasks to threadpools
- [ ] Add (optional?) user-defined names to threadpools
- [ ] Add docs
- [ ] Add tests with extra threadpool
- [x] Remove `Threads.resize_nthreads!` (ref https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42309)

runtime","user":{"login":"jpsamaroo","id":17912843,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE3OTEyODQz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/17912843?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo","html_url":"https://github.com/jpsamaroo","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"This PR implements threadpool support in the runtime, allowing extra threadpools of arbitrary sizes to be created at startup. The existing set of threads (specified by `-t` or `JULIA_NUM_THREADS`) are placed in threadpool 1, and subsequent threadpools (specified by `-T`, `--threadpools`, or `JULIA_THREADPOOLS` as a comma-separated list of threadpool sizes) get their own increasing integer ID. The approach I'm working towards is having threadpools act independently of each other, having dedicated PARTR queues, so that tasks placed in one threadpool do not migrate into other threadpools without user intervention.

I've tried to make the changes as non-breaking as possible, although it's definitely possible that users are relying on `Threads.threadid()` and `Threads.nthreads()` to be absolute w.r.t the total number of threads.

Todo:
- [x] Fix task placement via `jl_set_task_tid` into non-default threadpool
- [x] Implement multiq threadpool isolation in PARTR
- [x] Validate GC works on extra threadpools
- [x] Implement control layer for assigning library tasks to threadpools
- [ ] Add (optional?) user-defined names to threadpools
- [ ] Add docs
- [ ] Add tests with extra threadpool
- [x] Remove `Threads.resize_nthreads!` (ref https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42309)

@vchuravy @vtjnash @tkf  I'd appreciate your reviews of this proposed approach and PR implements threadpool support in the runtime, allowing extra threadpools of arbitrary sizes to be created at startup. The existing set of threads (specified by `-t` or `JULIA_NUM_THREADS`) are placed in threadpool 1, and subsequent threadpools (specified by `-T`, `--threadpools`, or `JULIA_THREADPOOLS` as a comma-separated list of threadpool sizes) get their own increasing integer ID. The approach I'm working towards is having threadpools act independently of each other, having dedicated PARTR queues, so that tasks placed in one threadpool do not migrate into other threadpools without user intervention.

I've tried to make the changes as non-breaking as possible, although it's definitely possible that users are relying on `Threads.threadid()` and `Threads.nthreads()` to be absolute w.r.t the total number of threads.

Todo:
- [x] Fix task placement via `jl_set_task_tid` into non-default threadpool
- [x] Implement multiq threadpool isolation in PARTR
- [x] Validate GC works on extra threadpools
- [x] Implement control layer for assigning library tasks to threadpools
- [ ] Add (optional?) user-defined names to threadpools
- [ ] Add docs
- [ ] Add tests with extra threadpool
- [x] Remove `Threads.resize_nthreads!` (ref https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42309)

@vchuravy @vtjnash @tkf  I'd appreciate your reviews of this proposed approach and While one can definitely argue that this code is bad because it's calling `jl_set_task_tid` manually, I do expect that this pattern is used elsewhere.

Basically, my hope is that once placed in a threadpool, code should be entirely unaware of other threadpools, and local initialization of per-thread buffers should be done based on the locally-available threads. Buffer initialization at the global level (at `__init__` time) will have to be adjusted to account for total threads in the session, and instead use `Threads.rawthreadid()` to access such buffers, which I think is fair given that they're trying to be non-local and need to have explicit knowledge of the size of all PR implements threadpool support in the runtime, allowing extra threadpools of arbitrary sizes to be created at startup. The existing set of threads (specified by `-t` or `JULIA_NUM_THREADS`) are placed in threadpool 1, and subsequent threadpools (specified by `-T`, `--threadpools`, or `JULIA_THREADPOOLS` as a comma-separated list of threadpool sizes) get their own increasing integer ID. The approach I'm working towards is having threadpools act independently of each other, having dedicated PARTR queues, so that tasks placed in one threadpool do not migrate into other threadpools without user intervention.

I've tried to make the changes as non-breaking as possible, although it's definitely possible that users are relying on `Threads.threadid()` and `Threads.nthreads()` to be absolute w.r.t the total number of threads.

Todo:
- [x] Fix task placement via `jl_set_task_tid` into non-default threadpool
- [x] Implement multiq threadpool isolation in PARTR
- [x] Validate GC works on extra threadpools
- [x] Implement control layer for assigning library tasks to threadpools
- [ ] Add (optional?) user-defined names to threadpools
- [ ] Add docs
- [ ] Add tests with extra threadpool
- [x] Remove `Threads.resize_nthreads!` (ref https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42309)

came up on slack and is just a RFC for now. There's likely more work needed especially in Pkg to fix broken assumptions.

This would allow checked-in environments to have manifest-pinned dependencies for different julia versions, without a manual pre-install step needed to rename a `Manifest1.8.toml` to `Manifest.toml` etc. like [JuliaRegistries/General](https://github.com/JuliaRegistries/General/blob/858129cfe1854de907ed0477a3943aa2caa35b4d/.github/workflows/ci.yml#L39-L41) does.

A downside of this approach is adding two file stat operations on-top of the most common scenarios of just having a Manifest.toml or no manifest saved at all, which might be quite expensive on some filesystems.

An alternative is to store multiple julia-versioned manifests within a single Manifest.toml

required"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":40,"created_at":"2021-09-18T18:42:07Z","updated_at":"2022-01-18T17:56:20Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/42302","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42302","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42302.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42302.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This PR implements threadpool support in the runtime, allowing extra threadpools of arbitrary sizes to be created at startup. The existing set of threads (specified by `-t` or `JULIA_NUM_THREADS`) are placed in threadpool 1, and subsequent threadpools (specified by `-T`, `--threadpools`, or `JULIA_THREADPOOLS` as a comma-separated list of threadpool sizes) get their own increasing integer ID. The approach I'm working towards is having threadpools act independently of each other, having dedicated PARTR queues, so that tasks placed in one threadpool do not migrate into other threadpools without user intervention.

I've tried to make the changes as non-breaking as possible, although it's definitely possible that users are relying on `Threads.threadid()` and `Threads.nthreads()` to be absolute w.r.t the total number of threads.

Todo:
- [x] Fix task placement via `jl_set_task_tid` into non-default threadpool
- [x] Implement multiq threadpool isolation in PARTR
- [x] Validate GC works on extra threadpools
- [x] Implement control layer for assigning library tasks to threadpools
- [ ] Add (optional?) user-defined names to threadpools
- [ ] Add docs
- [ ] Add tests with extra threadpool
- [x] Remove `Threads.resize_nthreads!` (ref https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42309)

@vchuravy @vtjnash @tkf  I'd appreciate your reviews of this proposed approach and implementation","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/42302/reactions","total_count":10,"+1":5,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":5,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/42302/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1015668889","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42302#issuecomment-1015668889","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/42302","id":1015668889,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM48ieCZ","user":{"login":"jpsamaroo","id":17912843,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE3OTEyODQz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/17912843?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo","html_url":"https://github.com/jpsamaroo","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-01-18T17:56:20Z","updated_at":"2022-01-18T17:56:20Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"Do we want to merge the new threadpool syntax with `-t`/`JULIA_NUM_THREADS`? runtime","user":{"login":"jpsamaroo","id":17912843,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE3OTEyODQz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/17912843?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo","html_url":"https://github.com/jpsamaroo","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"This PR implements threadpool support in the runtime, allowing extra threadpools of arbitrary sizes to be created at startup. The existing set of threads (specified by `-t` or `JULIA_NUM_THREADS`) are placed in threadpool 1, and subsequent threadpools (specified by `-T`, `--threadpools`, or `JULIA_THREADPOOLS` as a comma-separated list of threadpool sizes) get their own increasing integer ID. The approach I'm working towards is having threadpools act independently of each other, having dedicated PARTR queues, so that tasks placed in one threadpool do not migrate into other threadpools without user intervention.

I've tried to make the changes as non-breaking as possible, although it's definitely possible that users are relying on `Threads.threadid()` and `Threads.nthreads()` to be absolute w.r.t the total number of threads.

Todo:
- [x] Fix task placement via `jl_set_task_tid` into non-default threadpool
- [x] Implement multiq threadpool isolation in PARTR
- [x] Validate GC works on extra threadpools
- [x] Implement control layer for assigning library tasks to threadpools
- [x] Add (optional?) user-defined names to threadpools
- [ ] Add docs
- [ ] Add tests with extra threadpool
- [x] Remove `Threads.resize_nthreads!` (ref https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42309)

@vchuravy @vtjnash @tkf  I'd appreciate your reviews of this proposed approach and runtime","user":{"login":"jpsamaroo","id":17912843,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE3OTEyODQz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/17912843?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo","html_url":"https://github.com/jpsamaroo","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"This PR implements threadpool support in the runtime, allowing extra threadpools of arbitrary sizes to be created at startup. The existing set of threads (specified by `-t` or `JULIA_NUM_THREADS`) are placed in threadpool 1, and subsequent threadpools (specified by `-T`, `--threadpools`, or `JULIA_THREADPOOLS` as a comma-separated list of threadpool sizes) get their own increasing integer ID. The approach I'm working towards is having threadpools act independently of each other, having dedicated PARTR queues, so that tasks placed in one threadpool do not migrate into other threadpools without user intervention.

I've tried to make the changes as non-breaking as possible, although it's definitely possible that users are relying on `Threads.threadid()` and `Threads.nthreads()` to be absolute w.r.t the total number of threads.

Todo:
- [x] Fix task placement via `jl_set_task_tid` into non-default threadpool
- [x] Implement multiq threadpool isolation in PARTR
- [x] Validate GC works on extra threadpools
- [x] Implement control layer for assigning library tasks to threadpools
- [x] Add (optional?) user-defined names to threadpools
- [ ] Add docs
- [ ] Add tests with extra threadpool
- [x] Remove `Threads.resize_nthreads!` (ref https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42309)

collector"},{"id":3242880567,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwzMjQyODgwNTY3","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/profiler","name":"profiler","color":"9A315A","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":27,"created_at":"2021-10-22T20:58:01Z","updated_at":"2022-01-20T04:22:41Z","closed_at":"2022-01-19T20:51:01Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/42768","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42768","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42768.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42768.patch","merged_at":"2022-01-19T20:51:01Z"},"body":"## Overview Record the type and stack of every allocation (or only at a given sample interval), and return as Julia objects. Alternate approach to existing alloc profiler PR: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/33467 Complementary to garbage profiler PR: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42658 (maybe there's some nice way to meld them) This may be reinventing the wheel from #33467, but I'm not sure why that one needs stuff like LLVM passes. I mimicked some stuff from it, but this was my attempt to get something up and running. Could easily be missing stuff. ## Usage: ```julia using Profile.Allocs res = Allocs.@profile sample_rate=0.001 my_func() prof = Allocs.fetch() # do something with `prof` ``` See also: https://github.com/JuliaPerf/PProf.jl/pull/46 for support for visualizing these. Here are two sample screenshots from analyzing all allocations during function compilation and evaluation: ```julia julia> Profile.Allocs.@profile sample_rate=1 begin @eval begin f() = 1; f() end end 1 julia> PProf.Allocs.to_pprof(Profile.Allocs.fetch(), from_c=false) "alloc-profile.pb.gz" ``` ## Output: Julia structs which can be used by downstream libraries. (See `PProf.jl`: https://github.com/JuliaPerf/PProf.jl/pull/46) ## Performance Recording the stack traces with LibUnwind for each allocation has non-trivial overhead, so the allocs profiler comes with a `sample_rate = 0.0001` that controls the probability of recording a sample for each allocation. With `sample_rate = 1`, this example benchmark which allocates heavily (`for i in 1:100 fi = Symbol("f$i") ; @eval begin $fi() = 3 ; $fi() end`) spent around 50% of time recording the stack traces (on my MacBook Pro): Since almost all the overhead is in `jl_unw_stepn()`, there's not much more that can be improved in this PR. This is why we let users control the sample rate, based on their particular workload. ## TODO: - [x] move decoding into Julia - [x] handle multiple threads - [x] take `record_allocated_value` frame off the top - [x] more robust solution for detecting non-pointer type tags (i.e. remove `TYPE_PTR_LOW_THRESHOLD`) - [x] make it into a real stdlib package (or fold into `Profile` package); ensure tests are running - [x] docs and news - [ ] in future PRs: capture remaining allocations (incl. ones that don't go through this codepath). filed as https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43688 Co-Authored-By: @NHDaly ","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/42768/reactions","total_count":7,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":7,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/42768/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1017107328","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42768#issuecomment-1017107328","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/42768","id":1017107328,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM48n9OA","user":{"login":"jpsamaroo","id":17912843,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE3OTEyODQz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/17912843?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo","html_url":"https://github.com/jpsamaroo","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-01-20T04:22:41Z","updated_at":"2022-01-20T04:22:41Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"I'm not sure how accessing the current task's TID is considered a safepoint?
