docstring"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":23,"created_at":"2020-05-11T00:27:41Z","updated_at":"2022-01-21T15:18:50Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/35833","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/35833","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/35833.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/35833.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This PR proposes *context variables* API that can be used to propagate context-dependent information across task boundaries.  It is conceptually similar to `task_local_storage` but with a main difference that it "copies" all key-value pairs to child `Task`s.  The context variables are conceptually similar to dynamically scoped variables.

## Motivations

There are several places context variables can be useful or required.

### `ENV`

It was proposed to use `task_local_storage` to fix thread-safety of `withenv` https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/34726#issuecomment-586138687 by maintaining task-local copy (or overlay) of `ENV`.  However, it would mean that the environment variables cannot cross task boundaries.  Context variable can fix this shortcoming.

### `@testset`

`@testset` uses `task_local_storage` to track current active test set.  However, following example does not work (prints `No tests`) because the information of the current test does not propagate to the child task:

```julia
@testset begin
    @sync @async @test true
end
```

### Logging

`Task` has the `logstate` field that propagates to child tasks; i.e., it works as a hard-coded context variable.  Once context variable handling is sufficiently matured, it may be possible to eliminate the special handling from `Task` and use a context variable for `Task`.  Furthermore, context variable allows users to develop logging-like interfaces.

### Custom worker pool abstraction

In #35757 and [Propagation of available/assigned worker-IDs in hierarchical computations? - Domains / Julia at Scale - JuliaLang](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/propagation-of-available-assigned-worker-ids-in-hierarchical-computations/38670), it was brought up that propagating "computation resources" (thread/process pools, etc.) across tasks and processes is required for implementing custom worker pool interfaces.

### Misc.

> Request-related data, such as security tokens and request data in web applications, language context for gettext, etc.
>
> --- [Rationale section in PEP 0567](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0567/#rationale)

It would be useful for implementing a better nestable progress information handling https://github.com/JunoLab/ProgressLogging.jl/pull/13#discussion_r371057967.

## Other languages

### Implicit context

* Python has [`contextvars`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/contextvars.html). See [PEP 567](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0567/) and [PEP 550](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0567/) for discussion.
* .NET has [`ExecutionContext`](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/api/system.threading.executioncontext?view=netcore-3.1).
* Kotlin has [`CoroutineContext`](https://kotlinlang.org/docs/reference/coroutines/coroutine-context-and-dispatchers.html).

### Explicit context

* Go has [`context`](https://blog.golang.org/context).

## Proposed design

I propose an API with the following basic usages.

* Declare context variable: `@contextvar x`
* Get the value: `x[]`
* Set the value: `x[] = value`

For a tutorial and the full reference API of the proposed design, see https://tkf.github.io/ContextVariablesX.jl/dev/

Internally, `@contextvar` creates an instance of `ContextVar` which is defined as

```julia
struct ContextVar{T}
    key::UUID
    default::T
    ...
end
```

Then `x[]` and `x[] = value` invoke `current_task().ctxvars[x.key]` and an "immutable version" of `current_task().ctxvars[x.key] = value`, respectively (roughly speaking).

This has a couple of nice properties:

1. `x[]` can be inferred
2. `x` is forced to be namespaced (i.e., it has to exist in some module name space.)
3. `x` can be backed up by an efficient concrete key type (e.g., UUID)
4. `x` allows small-size optimization when the value type can be inlined into the context storage (in principle)

See also https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/35757 which already contains some discussion on this API.

I suspect the best way to get this implemented is:
- Have libraries try out/depend on https://github.com/tkf/ContextVariablesX.jl (for example: https://github.com/JuliaParallel/Dagger.jl/pull/328)
- Wire-up a smaller dictionary (HAMT?) in ContextVariablesX for small sets of context vars
- Benchmark this approach on all sorts of things
- If all went well, update this PR with that approach

I'm not sure what to do about making context vars optimizer-friendly; that seems like a can of worms best deferred until we're happy with the API and overhead of boxed context vars.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1018603019/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Naively, we could look at the rate of tasks being created vs. those same tasks running. In the absence of yield points, we should expect to see (on the invoking thread, or relative to the ultimate parent task) a high rate of task creation, without any intervening task pauses, followed by pauses of the ultimate parent task as it unwinds back to the original callsite.

In the presence of yield points, we should see low rate of task creation relative to task pauses on that invoking thread.

I was able to experimentally confirm this with this PR and `bpftrace`:

`sudo bpftrace -p 996338 -e 'usdt:usr/lib/libjulia-internal.so:julia:rt__new__task /arg0==0x00007f524d088010/{ printf("Task CREATE: %x\n", arg0); } usdt:usr/lib/libjulia-internal.so:julia:rt__run__task {printf("Task RUN: %x\n", arg0);}'`

With `f === identity`, `op === +`:

```

```

With `f === identity`, `op === function add_yield(x,y)
println(devnull, "1")
x+y
end`:

```

docstring"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":27,"created_at":"2020-05-11T00:27:41Z","updated_at":"2022-01-24T16:49:59Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/35833","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/35833","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/35833.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/35833.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This PR proposes *context variables* API that can be used to propagate context-dependent information across task boundaries.  It is conceptually similar to `task_local_storage` but with a main difference that it "copies" all key-value pairs to child `Task`s.  The context variables are conceptually similar to dynamically scoped variables.

## Motivations

There are several places context variables can be useful or required.

### `ENV`

It was proposed to use `task_local_storage` to fix thread-safety of `withenv` https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/34726#issuecomment-586138687 by maintaining task-local copy (or overlay) of `ENV`.  However, it would mean that the environment variables cannot cross task boundaries.  Context variable can fix this shortcoming.

### `@testset`

`@testset` uses `task_local_storage` to track current active test set.  However, following example does not work (prints `No tests`) because the information of the current test does not propagate to the child task:

```julia
@testset begin
    @sync @async @test true
end
```

### Logging

`Task` has the `logstate` field that propagates to child tasks; i.e., it works as a hard-coded context variable.  Once context variable handling is sufficiently matured, it may be possible to eliminate the special handling from `Task` and use a context variable for `Task`.  Furthermore, context variable allows users to develop logging-like interfaces.

### Custom worker pool abstraction

In #35757 and [Propagation of available/assigned worker-IDs in hierarchical computations? - Domains / Julia at Scale - JuliaLang](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/propagation-of-available-assigned-worker-ids-in-hierarchical-computations/38670), it was brought up that propagating "computation resources" (thread/process pools, etc.) across tasks and processes is required for implementing custom worker pool interfaces.

### Misc.

> Request-related data, such as security tokens and request data in web applications, language context for gettext, etc.
>
> --- [Rationale section in PEP 0567](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0567/#rationale)

It would be useful for implementing a better nestable progress information handling https://github.com/JunoLab/ProgressLogging.jl/pull/13#discussion_r371057967.

## Other languages

### Implicit context

* Python has [`contextvars`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/contextvars.html). See [PEP 567](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0567/) and [PEP 550](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0567/) for discussion.
* .NET has [`ExecutionContext`](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/api/system.threading.executioncontext?view=netcore-3.1).
* Kotlin has [`CoroutineContext`](https://kotlinlang.org/docs/reference/coroutines/coroutine-context-and-dispatchers.html).

### Explicit context

* Go has [`context`](https://blog.golang.org/context).

## Proposed design

I propose an API with the following basic usages.

* Declare context variable: `@contextvar x`
* Get the value: `x[]`
* Set the value: `x[] = value`

For a tutorial and the full reference API of the proposed design, see https://tkf.github.io/ContextVariablesX.jl/dev/

Internally, `@contextvar` creates an instance of `ContextVar` which is defined as

```julia
struct ContextVar{T}
    key::UUID
    default::T
    ...
end
```

Then `x[]` and `x[] = value` invoke `current_task().ctxvars[x.key]` and an "immutable version" of `current_task().ctxvars[x.key] = value`, respectively (roughly speaking).

This has a couple of nice properties:

1. `x[]` can be inferred
2. `x` is forced to be namespaced (i.e., it has to exist in some module name space.)
3. `x` can be backed up by an efficient concrete key type (e.g., UUID)
4. `x` allows small-size optimization when the value type can be inlined into the context storage (in principle)

See also https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/35757 which already contains some discussion on this API.

`Threads.@threads`","user":{"login":"IanButterworth","id":1694067,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE2OTQwNjc=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1694067?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth","html_url":"https://github.com/IanButterworth","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"In a conversation with @tkf and @jpsamaroo this idea came up, to enable a more flexibly scheduled variant of `Threads.@threads` via a new `:dynamic` schedule strategy. (I offered to PR it)

An example where `busywait` is a non-yielding timed loop that waits for a number of seconds.
Note that `:static` is the current default where no schedule is specified:

```julia
julia> @time begin
            Threads.@spawn busywait(5)
            Threads.@threads :static for i in 1:Threads.nthreads()
                busywait(1)
            end
        end
6.003001 seconds (16.33 k allocations: 899.255 KiB, 0.25% compilation time)

julia> @time begin
            Threads.@spawn busywait(5)
            Threads.@threads :dynamic for i in 1:Threads.nthreads()
                busywait(1)
            end
        end
2.012056 seconds (16.05 k allocations: 883.919 KiB, 0.66% compilation time)
```

Unlike `:static` the `:dynamic` strategy can be nested.

```julia
julia> @time begin
        Threads.@spawn busywait(5)
        Threads.@threads :dynamic for i in 1:Threads.nthreads()
            Threads.@threads :dynamic for j in 1:Threads.nthreads()
                busywait(1)
            end
        end
    end
 17.023767 seconds (61.46 k allocations: 3.402 MiB, 0.14% compilation time)

julia> Threads.nthreads()
16
```

@vtjnash: A thought was that the threaded region didn't need to be marked with this strategy, is that correct?

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/comments/791817813","pull_request_review_id":862429529,"id":791817813,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOABkWpM4vMi5V","diff_hunk":"@@ -110,15 +112,67 @@ A barrier is placed at the end of the loop which waits for all tasks to finish
 execution.
 
 The `schedule` argument can be used to request a particular scheduling policy.
-The only currently supported value is `:static`, which creates one task per thread
-and divides the iterations equally among them. Specifying `:static` is an error
-if used from inside another `@threads` loop or from a thread other than 1.
+
+Except for `:static` scheduling, how the iterations are assigned to tasks, and how the tasks
+are assigned to the worker threads are undefined. The exact assignments can be different
+for each execution. The scheduling option should be considered as a hint. The loop body
+code (including any code transitively called from it) must not assume the task and worker
+thread in which they are executed. The loop body code for each iteration must be able to
+make forward progress and be free from data races independent of the state of other iterations.
+As such, synchronizations across iterations may invoke deadlock.
+
+For example, the above conditions imply that:
+
+- The lock taken in an iteration must be released within the same iteration.
+- Avoid communication between iterations using, e.g., Channels.","path":"base/threadingconstructs.jl","position":68,"original_position":68,"commit_id":"5cb286aff512e1917614b52fff8b6d9f5daeadfd","original_commit_id":"5cb286aff512e1917614b52fff8b6d9f5daeadfd","user":{"login":"jpsamaroo","id":17912843,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE3OTEyODQz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/17912843?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo","html_url":"https://github.com/jpsamaroo","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"```suggestion

`Threads.@threads`","user":{"login":"IanButterworth","id":1694067,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE2OTQwNjc=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1694067?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth","html_url":"https://github.com/IanButterworth","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"In a conversation with @tkf and @jpsamaroo this idea came up, to enable a more flexibly scheduled variant of `Threads.@threads` via a new `:dynamic` schedule strategy. (I offered to PR it)

An example where `busywait` is a non-yielding timed loop that waits for a number of seconds.
Note that `:static` is the current default where no schedule is specified:

```julia
julia> @time begin
            Threads.@spawn busywait(5)
            Threads.@threads :static for i in 1:Threads.nthreads()
                busywait(1)
            end
        end
6.003001 seconds (16.33 k allocations: 899.255 KiB, 0.25% compilation time)

julia> @time begin
            Threads.@spawn busywait(5)
            Threads.@threads :dynamic for i in 1:Threads.nthreads()
                busywait(1)
            end
        end
2.012056 seconds (16.05 k allocations: 883.919 KiB, 0.66% compilation time)
```

Unlike `:static` the `:dynamic` strategy can be nested.

```julia
julia> @time begin
        Threads.@spawn busywait(5)
        Threads.@threads :dynamic for i in 1:Threads.nthreads()
            Threads.@threads :dynamic for j in 1:Threads.nthreads()
                busywait(1)
            end
        end
    end
 17.023767 seconds (61.46 k allocations: 3.402 MiB, 0.14% compilation time)

julia> Threads.nthreads()
16
```

@vtjnash: A thought was that the threaded region didn't need to be marked with this strategy, is that correct?

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/comments/791818094","pull_request_review_id":862429529,"id":791818094,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOABkWpM4vMi9u","diff_hunk":"@@ -110,15 +112,67 @@ A barrier is placed at the end of the loop which waits for all tasks to finish
 execution.
 
 The `schedule` argument can be used to request a particular scheduling policy.
-The only currently supported value is `:static`, which creates one task per thread
-and divides the iterations equally among them. Specifying `:static` is an error
-if used from inside another `@threads` loop or from a thread other than 1.
+
+Except for `:static` scheduling, how the iterations are assigned to tasks, and how the tasks
+are assigned to the worker threads are undefined. The exact assignments can be different
+for each execution. The scheduling option should be considered as a hint. The loop body
+code (including any code transitively called from it) must not assume the task and worker
+thread in which they are executed. The loop body code for each iteration must be able to
+make forward progress and be free from data races independent of the state of other iterations.
+As such, synchronizations across iterations may invoke deadlock.
+
+For example, the above conditions imply that:
+
+- The lock taken in an iteration must be released within the same iteration.
+- Avoid communication between iterations using, e.g., Channels.
+- Write only to locations not shared across iterations (unless lock or atomic operation is used).","path":"base/threadingconstructs.jl","position":69,"original_position":69,"commit_id":"5cb286aff512e1917614b52fff8b6d9f5daeadfd","original_commit_id":"5cb286aff512e1917614b52fff8b6d9f5daeadfd","user":{"login":"jpsamaroo","id":17912843,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE3OTEyODQz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/17912843?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo","html_url":"https://github.com/jpsamaroo","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"```suggestion

`Threads.@threads`","user":{"login":"IanButterworth","id":1694067,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE2OTQwNjc=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1694067?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth","html_url":"https://github.com/IanButterworth","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"In a conversation with @tkf and @jpsamaroo this idea came up, to enable a more flexibly scheduled variant of `Threads.@threads` via a new `:dynamic` schedule strategy. (I offered to PR it)

An example where `busywait` is a non-yielding timed loop that waits for a number of seconds.
Note that `:static` is the current default where no schedule is specified:

```julia
julia> @time begin
            Threads.@spawn busywait(5)
            Threads.@threads :static for i in 1:Threads.nthreads()
                busywait(1)
            end
        end
6.003001 seconds (16.33 k allocations: 899.255 KiB, 0.25% compilation time)

julia> @time begin
            Threads.@spawn busywait(5)
            Threads.@threads :dynamic for i in 1:Threads.nthreads()
                busywait(1)
            end
        end
2.012056 seconds (16.05 k allocations: 883.919 KiB, 0.66% compilation time)
```

Unlike `:static` the `:dynamic` strategy can be nested.

```julia
julia> @time begin
        Threads.@spawn busywait(5)
        Threads.@threads :dynamic for i in 1:Threads.nthreads()
            Threads.@threads :dynamic for j in 1:Threads.nthreads()
                busywait(1)
            end
        end
    end
 17.023767 seconds (61.46 k allocations: 3.402 MiB, 0.14% compilation time)

julia> Threads.nthreads()
16
```

@vtjnash: A thought was that the threaded region didn't need to be marked with this strategy, is that correct?

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/comments/791820991","pull_request_review_id":862429529,"id":791820991,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOABkWpM4vMjq_","diff_hunk":"@@ -110,15 +112,67 @@ A barrier is placed at the end of the loop which waits for all tasks to finish
 execution.
 
 The `schedule` argument can be used to request a particular scheduling policy.
-The only currently supported value is `:static`, which creates one task per thread
-and divides the iterations equally among them. Specifying `:static` is an error
-if used from inside another `@threads` loop or from a thread other than 1.
+
+Except for `:static` scheduling, how the iterations are assigned to tasks, and how the tasks
+are assigned to the worker threads are undefined. The exact assignments can be different
+for each execution. The scheduling option should be considered as a hint. The loop body
+code (including any code transitively called from it) must not assume the task and worker
+thread in which they are executed. The loop body code for each iteration must be able to
+make forward progress and be free from data races independent of the state of other iterations.
+As such, synchronizations across iterations may invoke deadlock.
+
+For example, the above conditions imply that:
+
+- The lock taken in an iteration must be released within the same iteration.
+- Avoid communication between iterations using, e.g., Channels.
+- Write only to locations not shared across iterations (unless lock or atomic operation is used).
+
+Furthermore, even though `lock` and atomic operations can be useful sometimes, it is often better
+to avoid them for performance.","path":"base/threadingconstructs.jl","position":72,"original_position":72,"commit_id":"5cb286aff512e1917614b52fff8b6d9f5daeadfd","original_commit_id":"5cb286aff512e1917614b52fff8b6d9f5daeadfd","user":{"login":"jpsamaroo","id":17912843,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE3OTEyODQz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/17912843?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo","html_url":"https://github.com/jpsamaroo","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"This confuses the purpose of this docstring, which is to explain the different scheduling hints and their behaviors/constraints. `Threads.@threads`","user":{"login":"IanButterworth","id":1694067,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE2OTQwNjc=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1694067?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth","html_url":"https://github.com/IanButterworth","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"In a conversation with @tkf and @jpsamaroo this idea came up, to enable a more flexibly scheduled variant of `Threads.@threads` via a new `:dynamic` schedule strategy. (I offered to PR it)

An example where `busywait` is a non-yielding timed loop that waits for a number of seconds.
Note that `:static` is the current default where no schedule is specified:

```julia
julia> @time begin
            Threads.@spawn busywait(5)
            Threads.@threads :static for i in 1:Threads.nthreads()
                busywait(1)
            end
        end
6.003001 seconds (16.33 k allocations: 899.255 KiB, 0.25% compilation time)

julia> @time begin
            Threads.@spawn busywait(5)
            Threads.@threads :dynamic for i in 1:Threads.nthreads()
                busywait(1)
            end
        end
2.012056 seconds (16.05 k allocations: 883.919 KiB, 0.66% compilation time)
```

Unlike `:static` the `:dynamic` strategy can be nested.

```julia
julia> @time begin
        Threads.@spawn busywait(5)
        Threads.@threads :dynamic for i in 1:Threads.nthreads()
            Threads.@threads :dynamic for j in 1:Threads.nthreads()
                busywait(1)
            end
        end
    end
 17.023767 seconds (61.46 k allocations: 3.402 MiB, 0.14% compilation time)

julia> Threads.nthreads()
16
```

@vtjnash: A thought was that the threaded region didn't need to be marked with this strategy, is that correct?

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/comments/791821608","pull_request_review_id":862429529,"id":791821608,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOABkWpM4vMj0o","diff_hunk":"@@ -110,15 +112,67 @@ A barrier is placed at the end of the loop which waits for all tasks to finish
 execution.
 
 The `schedule` argument can be used to request a particular scheduling policy.
-The only currently supported value is `:static`, which creates one task per thread
-and divides the iterations equally among them. Specifying `:static` is an error
-if used from inside another `@threads` loop or from a thread other than 1.
+
+Except for `:static` scheduling, how the iterations are assigned to tasks, and how the tasks
+are assigned to the worker threads are undefined. The exact assignments can be different
+for each execution. The scheduling option should be considered as a hint. The loop body
+code (including any code transitively called from it) must not assume the task and worker
+thread in which they are executed. The loop body code for each iteration must be able to
+make forward progress and be free from data races independent of the state of other iterations.
+As such, synchronizations across iterations may invoke deadlock.
+
+For example, the above conditions imply that:
+
+- The lock taken in an iteration must be released within the same iteration.
+- Avoid communication between iterations using, e.g., Channels.
+- Write only to locations not shared across iterations (unless lock or atomic operation is used).
+
+Furthermore, even though `lock` and atomic operations can be useful sometimes, it is often better
+to avoid them for performance.
+
+Schedule options are:
+- `:static` which creates one task per thread and divides the iterations equally among","path":"base/threadingconstructs.jl","position":75,"original_position":75,"commit_id":"5cb286aff512e1917614b52fff8b6d9f5daeadfd","original_commit_id":"5cb286aff512e1917614b52fff8b6d9f5daeadfd","user":{"login":"jpsamaroo","id":17912843,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE3OTEyODQz","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/17912843?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo","html_url":"https://github.com/jpsamaroo","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jpsamaroo/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"```suggestion

a conversation with @tkf and @jpsamaroo this idea came up, to enable a more flexibly scheduled variant of `Threads.@threads` via a new `:dynamic` schedule strategy. (I offered to PR it)

An example where `busywait` is a non-yielding timed loop that waits for a number of seconds.
Note that `:static` is the current default where no schedule is specified:

```julia
julia> @time begin
            Threads.@spawn busywait(5)
            Threads.@threads :static for i in 1:Threads.nthreads()
                busywait(1)
            end
        end
6.003001 seconds (16.33 k allocations: 899.255 KiB, 0.25% compilation time)

julia> @time begin
            Threads.@spawn busywait(5)
            Threads.@threads :dynamic for i in 1:Threads.nthreads()
                busywait(1)
            end
        end
2.012056 seconds (16.05 k allocations: 883.919 KiB, 0.66% compilation time)
```

Unlike `:static` the `:dynamic` strategy can be nested.

```julia
julia> @time begin
        Threads.@spawn busywait(5)
        Threads.@threads :dynamic for i in 1:Threads.nthreads()
            Threads.@threads :dynamic for j in 1:Threads.nthreads()
                busywait(1)
            end
        end
    end
 17.023767 seconds (61.46 k allocations: 3.402 MiB, 0.14% compilation time)

julia> Threads.nthreads()
16
```

@vtjnash: A thought was that the threaded region didn't need to be marked with this strategy, is that correct?

 execution.
 
 The `schedule` argument can be used to request a particular scheduling policy.
-The only currently supported value is `:static`, which creates one task per thread
-and divides the iterations equally among them. Specifying `:static` is an error
-if used from inside another `@threads` loop or from a thread other than 1.
+
+Except for `:static` scheduling, how the iterations are assigned to tasks, and how the tasks
+are assigned to the worker threads are undefined. The exact assignments can be different
+for each execution. The scheduling option should be considered as a hint. The loop body
+code (including any code transitively called from it) must not assume the task and worker
+thread in which they are executed. The loop body code for each iteration must be able to
+make forward progress and be free from data races independent of the state of other iterations.
+As such, synchronizations across iterations may invoke deadlock.
+
+For example, the above conditions imply that:
+
+- The lock taken in an iteration must be released within the same iteration.
+- Avoid communication between iterations using, e.g., Channels.
+- Write only to locations not shared across iterations (unless lock or atomic operation is used).
+
+Furthermore, even though `lock` and atomic operations can be useful sometimes, it is often better
+to avoid them for performance.
+
+Schedule options are:
+- `:static` which creates one task per thread and divides the iterations equally among
+            them, assigning each task specifically to each thread.
+            Specifying `:static` is an error if used from inside another `@threads` loop
+            or from a thread other than 1.
+- `:dynamic` tries to schedule iterations dynamically to available worker threads,
+            assuming that the workload for each iteration is uniform.
+
+If no schedule is specified, when called from thread 1 the default is `:static`, or when
+called from other threads the loop will be executed without threading.
 
 The default schedule (used when no `schedule` argument is present) is subject to change.
 
+For example, an illustration of the different scheduling strategies where `busywait`
+is a non-yielding timed loop that runs for a number of seconds.
+

a conversation with @tkf and @jpsamaroo this idea came up, to enable a more flexibly scheduled variant of `Threads.@threads` via a new `:dynamic` schedule strategy. (I offered to PR it)

An example where `busywait` is a non-yielding timed loop that waits for a number of seconds.
Note that `:static` is the current default where no schedule is specified:

```julia
julia> @time begin
            Threads.@spawn busywait(5)
            Threads.@threads :static for i in 1:Threads.nthreads()
                busywait(1)
            end
        end
6.003001 seconds (16.33 k allocations: 899.255 KiB, 0.25% compilation time)

julia> @time begin
            Threads.@spawn busywait(5)
            Threads.@threads :dynamic for i in 1:Threads.nthreads()
                busywait(1)
            end
        end
2.012056 seconds (16.05 k allocations: 883.919 KiB, 0.66% compilation time)
```

Unlike `:static` the `:dynamic` strategy can be nested.

```julia
julia> @time begin
        Threads.@spawn busywait(5)
        Threads.@threads :dynamic for i in 1:Threads.nthreads()
            Threads.@threads :dynamic for j in 1:Threads.nthreads()
                busywait(1)
            end
        end
    end
 17.023767 seconds (61.46 k allocations: 3.402 MiB, 0.14% compilation time)

julia> Threads.nthreads()
16
```

@vtjnash: A thought was that the threaded region didn't need to be marked with this strategy, is that correct?

`Threads.@threads`","user":{"login":"IanButterworth","id":1694067,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE2OTQwNjc=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1694067?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth","html_url":"https://github.com/IanButterworth","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"In a conversation with @tkf and @jpsamaroo this idea came up, to enable a more flexibly scheduled variant of `Threads.@threads` via a new `:dynamic` schedule strategy. (I offered to PR it)

An example where `busywait` is a non-yielding timed loop that waits for a number of seconds.
Note that `:static` is the current default where no schedule is specified:

```julia
julia> @time begin
            Threads.@spawn busywait(5)
            Threads.@threads :static for i in 1:Threads.nthreads()
                busywait(1)
            end
        end
6.003001 seconds (16.33 k allocations: 899.255 KiB, 0.25% compilation time)

julia> @time begin
            Threads.@spawn busywait(5)
            Threads.@threads :dynamic for i in 1:Threads.nthreads()
                busywait(1)
            end
        end
2.012056 seconds (16.05 k allocations: 883.919 KiB, 0.66% compilation time)
```

Unlike `:static` the `:dynamic` strategy can be nested.

```julia
julia> @time begin
        Threads.@spawn busywait(5)
        Threads.@threads :dynamic for i in 1:Threads.nthreads()
            Threads.@threads :dynamic for j in 1:Threads.nthreads()
                busywait(1)
            end
        end
    end
 17.023767 seconds (61.46 k allocations: 3.402 MiB, 0.14% compilation time)

julia> Threads.nthreads()
16
```

@vtjnash: A thought was that the threaded region didn't need to be marked with this strategy, is that correct?

`Threads.@threads`","user":{"login":"IanButterworth","id":1694067,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE2OTQwNjc=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1694067?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth","html_url":"https://github.com/IanButterworth","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/IanButterworth/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"In a conversation with @tkf and @jpsamaroo this idea came up, to enable a more flexibly scheduled variant of `Threads.@threads` via a new `:dynamic` schedule strategy. (I offered to PR it)

An example where `busywait` is a non-yielding timed loop that waits for a number of seconds.
Note that `:static` is the current default where no schedule is specified:

```julia
julia> @time begin
            Threads.@spawn busywait(5)
            Threads.@threads :static for i in 1:Threads.nthreads()
                busywait(1)
            end
        end
6.003001 seconds (16.33 k allocations: 899.255 KiB, 0.25% compilation time)

julia> @time begin
            Threads.@spawn busywait(5)
            Threads.@threads :dynamic for i in 1:Threads.nthreads()
                busywait(1)
            end
        end
2.012056 seconds (16.05 k allocations: 883.919 KiB, 0.66% compilation time)
```

Unlike `:static` the `:dynamic` strategy can be nested.

```julia
julia> @time begin
        Threads.@spawn busywait(5)
        Threads.@threads :dynamic for i in 1:Threads.nthreads()
            Threads.@threads :dynamic for j in 1:Threads.nthreads()
                busywait(1)
            end
        end
    end
 17.023767 seconds (61.46 k allocations: 3.402 MiB, 0.14% compilation time)

julia> Threads.nthreads()
16
```

@vtjnash: A thought was that the threaded region didn't need to be marked with this strategy, is that correct?

this PR, if the Julia binary was built from a tagged commit, then `InteractiveUtils.versioninfo` will print the tagged release banner.

For example, for the official release binaries, the tagged release banner is:
- `Official https://julialang.org/ release`.

## Motivation

Currently, when people post (e.g. on Discourse) about a problem that they are having, we ask them to post the output of `import InteractiveUtils; InteractiveUtils.versioninfo()` so that we know what version of Julia they are using.

Another common question that we ask is "are you using the official binaries from the Julia website?"

By printing the contents of the tagged release banner as part of the output from `InteractiveUtils.versioninfo()`, we can easily figure out whether someone is using the official binaries, or if they installed Julia some other
